text: **Mao Ting Ke Hua (Tales of the Thatched Pavilion Guest) by Huang Xiufu** **Volume One** **Omens of Shu** In the second year of the Gande era during the Song Dynasty, the year being the Jiazi cycle, the imperial forces mobilized to attack Shu. The following spring, the ruler of Shu surrendered. In February, Lu Gong Yuqing, a high-ranking official, was appointed to oversee military affairs, and the former royal prince's office of merit was designated as the administrative center. Previously, the ruler of Shu had the custom of giving peach wood tablets to the palace gates each New Year's Eve, inscribed with the phrases "Yuan," "Heng," "Li," and "Zheng." The former royal prince, skilled in calligraphy, selected a tablet from his own office and inscribed it with the words "Tian Chui Yu Qing" (Heaven bestows abundant blessings) and "Di Jie Chang Chun" (Earth connects with eternal spring), which were considered auspicious and elegant. At this time, Lu Gong's name was Yuqing, and the emperor's birthday celebration was named "Changchun," symbolizing longevity. The phrases "Tian Chui" and "Di Jie" clearly indicated the omens. The rise and fall of a nation is indeed predetermined by fate. **Auspicious Sign from Wood** In the later years of the Guangzheng era during the Later Shu Dynasty, a man named Tang Jiming, whose father's name is unknown, in Chengdu, cut open a piece of wood and found purple-colored cursive script inscribed with the words "Taiping" (Prosperity). At the time, he intended to present this as an auspicious sign to the ruler of Shu. However, a knowledgeable person explained that true peace would not come until the fall of the Shu state. Indeed, after the Song Dynasty's campaign, frequent acts of clemency were issued, and the harsh customs of the war-torn period were gradually abolished. The era name was later changed to "Taiping Xingguo" (Prosperity and Nation-Building), confirming the wisdom of the knowledgeable person's interpretation.

**The Sweet Dew** In the Wu Shen year of the Holy Song dynasty, the Emperor performed the *Yuanfu* rites at Mount Tai. At that time, the grace of rain and dew was universally bestowed upon all the land, and the entire realm was granted the privilege of *da pu*—a period of public celebration. In the tenth month of that winter, Governor and Privy Council Official Ren Zhongzheng, in front of the southern hall of the government office, held a grand banquet with music, dancers, and entertainers to honor the elderly, in accordance with the imperial edict. The grandeur of this celebration was such that even the aged and elderly of Shu had never before witnessed anything like it; it was indeed a sight of great honor. The cheers and shouts from the crowd shook the surrounding regions, and all exclaimed that in previous years they had twice fallen into the hands of bandits, suffering great hardship, but who could have imagined that today they would encounter a civilized ruler, live in peace, and witness this golden age? In the twelfth month of that year, the sweet dew fell upon eight locations: the Great Holy Mercy Temple, the Sweet Dew Temple, the Pure Assembly Temple, the Golden Rope Institute, the Dragon Ascendance Temple, the Green Sheep Palace, and the Daoist hall within the government office. From the *Chengtian Jie* to the twentieth day, it rained continuously upon the bamboo and cypress trees every night, covering every leaf, regardless of size, entirely. Scholars and commoners alike carried the elderly and children and rushed along the roads, using bowls and vessels to collect and taste it; the drink was as sweet as honey. The local Confucian scholar who compiled the *Book of Auspicious Omens* said: "The descent of sweet dew occurs when the ruler honors the wise and respects the elderly, and thus the bamboo and cypress trees receive it. When a sage acts in accordance with the Dao, and virtue moves the heavens and earth, producing a response, it is called a *ruì*—an omen of good fortune. Is this not precisely what we are witnessing?"

The Divine Lord's Wooden Pillar In the sixth year of the Dazhong Xiangfu era (1013 AD), in the Chongxian Temple of Changming County, Mianzhou, there was a wooden pillar with wood grain resembling a painting of the Heavenly Lord, with every detail of hair, eyebrows, eyes, clothing, shoes, and even the finest threads fully depicted. The governor, Liu Gong Zongyan, a deputy magistrate, then painted the image and reported it to the court. The imperial decree ordered it to be transported to the capital and sent to the Jade Clear Palace of Response. The temple's abbot was bestowed purple robes and gifts of tea and silk. Now, the people of Sichuan all paint and worship it. The Disappearance of Tigers and Bandits Before the Holy Court regained control over Shu, the tigers in the region between Jian and Li were especially fierce. On White Guard Mountain and Stone Tube Ravine, there was a tiger named "Fur Coated," and the place was called "Tax Man Field." In the White Poplar Forest between Mian and Han, there was a tiger named "Split Hoof." Merchants and travelers gathered in groups to travel, but many were attacked and devoured. On Cow Neck Mountain in Jiuzhou, there were mother and cub tigers, and on Iron Furnace Mountain in Lingzhou, there was a green leopard. In the nearby mountains of Peng and Shu, fierce animals formed packs. Farmers dared not let their livestock graze, and those who went out to gather firewood and travelers all suffered from them. There were also bandits in various states and counties, gathered in groups of one hundred to two hundred people. When government troops tried to capture them, they fought back. If they won, they would attack the government troops; if they lost, they would flee into the forests. Even though there were officials tasked with capturing bandits, none could catch them. For nearly forty years, the people had no stable livelihood. After the Holy Court regained control, annual tributes, official missions, merchants, and travelers traveled day and night in succession. Houses were built side by side, and dogs and pigs roamed freely. Tigers, leopards, and bandits all disappeared. Is this not due to the prosperity or decline of the state and the effectiveness of its policies?

Shu has no great flood. In the fifth year of Kai Bao, during the Ren Shen year, in the eighth month, heavy rain fell in Chengdu, causing the Min River to surge. The great dam in Yongkang Military District was about to collapse, and water entered the capital river. The military governor Xue She, with his people, was filled with anxiety. They could only see the turbulent waves and roaring sounds like thunder, with waves reaching a height of ten zhang. In the middle of the river, there was a large object floating downstream with the waves. Upon closer inspection, it was a large snake. The snake raised its head and lay horizontally across the dam. That night, they heard the sounds of shouting on the dam, with torches arranged in all directions. Despite the strong wind and heavy rain, the fire did not extinguish. At dawn, the flags in the Guangji Temple of Duke Li were all wet, and on the dam, only one flag remained, with the water from the sand dike entering the mouth of the Xinzhen River. At that time, Jiamian Prefecture suffered severe flooding, but the capital river did not overflow. Originally, Li Bing, during the Qin Dynasty, served as the governor of Shu alongside Zhang Ruo, a man of virtue. Shu had long been plagued by water disasters, with frogs appearing in kitchen stoves and people suffering from flooding. Duke Li used Daoist methods to control water spirits and capture water monsters, thereby stopping the surging waves. He carved mountains to create barriers and diverted the Min River into two rivers, irrigating the lands of Peng and Han. The three prefectures of Shu became fertile with millions of acres of land. He also created three stone figures to swear to the river, saying: "May the water's rise and fall never be too high or too low, never overflowing the shoulders." He made five stone rhinoceroses to subdue water spirits. Thus, Shu became a fertile land, free from water disasters. The wells and fields were prosperous, and his virtues and deeds have not faded, and people still rely on them today. It is said that his water management skills were comparable to those of Yu the Great. Without his achievements, the people would have been fish. Along the rivers, temples were built to honor him.

The Tracks of the Carriage On Mount Luoxiu in Luojiang County, Mianzhou, there is a cave known as the Luoxiu Cave. It is said that the legendary Taoist master Luo Qiu, whose name was also Luoxiu, practiced his Daoist arts and ascended to immortality here. This cave is believed to have the power to influence natural phenomena such as floods, droughts, and epidemics, and prayers made to it are always answered. In the fifth year of the Tianping era (980 AD) during the Mid-Autumn Festival, the air was filled with colorful mist and light smoke, the moonlight as bright as day, and fragrant winds carrying auspicious vapors spread throughout the valleys. Villagers from far and wide climbed high hills to observe this extraordinary scene, hearing only the sounds of music and the jingling of jade pendants. By dawn, they saw the tracks of a carriage, stretching for more than ten miles from the cave, about a hundred feet wide, and crushing the soil to a depth of three or four inches. The tracks followed the contours of the mountain, gradually narrowing until they reached the cave entrance, where they became smaller. It was unclear whether these were the tracks of a divine being who had driven out of the cave. The sound of music never ceased, day or night, and soon it was heard throughout the various states and counties. At this time, Chen Tan, the imperial court official and county magistrate, and Zou Chongrang, the county sheriff, went to the fairy cave to observe these tracks and the music, and reported the matter to the emperor.

The emperor decreed that the respected Taoist master Li Dongbin, known as "Tiger Ear Master" and residing on Mount Da Jiujing, should carry incense to the entrance of the cave. He was to set up a ritual of fumigation and inspect the cave, praying for divine favor. Li Dongbin, a man known as "Li Eight Hundred" due to his reputed age of 800 years, though appearing to be around 50, had a childlike face and dense black hair. He walked quickly but spoke slowly. When he paused, scholars and common people gathered around him like a wall. The master would take out a few copper coins from his sleeves and scatter them, causing the crowd to disperse slightly. He would do this every ten to twenty steps, and by evening, his sleeves and pockets remained full of coins. The next day, accompanied by officials, he entered the cave. After walking for about ten miles, they only heard a faint, fragrant aroma and distant music. The officials and attendants held incense and candles, breathing cautiously as they climbed over vines and rocky paths, their souls trembling and sweat dripping. Despite the eerie atmosphere, the master walked steadily and calmly. When they emerged from the cave, his clothes and shoes were free of any dirt or stains.

Mr. Junyou Cheng From the village of Xianyu Ya in Shicheng Town, Xiaoxi County, Suizhou, there was a villager named Mr. Junyou Cheng. He had a few family members and tilled and farmed with great effort, often hiring out his labor to weave straw sandals to support himself. He was plain and simple in nature but kind and benevolent. When he saw animals, he would always step aside to avoid them, not wanting to disturb them. He was shy and spoke little with others. At the age of sixty, whenever he saw hermits or Taoist priests gathering, he would hire himself out to carry their belongings and receive payment to serve them. Whenever someone had luggage, he would carry it for them, regardless of distance. If they left him money, he would not look back. He did this regularly. In the spring of the ninth year of Kai Bao (976 AD), he went to Yunding Mountain Temple and met a Taoist priest. The priest had an ancient appearance and divine grace, wearing a simple robe and a coarse headband, leading a black dog. He said to Mr. Junyou, "I wish you would carry my walking stick and medicine bag to Qingcheng Mountain, and I will pay you double for your trouble." Mr. Junyou gladly followed him, and they entered a small path.

At first, the fields were overgrown, but gradually, flowers and trees came into view, and the path was slightly different from the usual one. After walking three to four miles, they saw strange rocks lining the path, all growing fine bamboo and peach blossoms, with flying springs and resonant sounds echoing in the valley. In the distance, a pavilion sat by the mountain and water, with pine and osmanthus being serene and quiet, and thin mist and light smoke fluttering on both sides. The black dog ran ahead, and the Taoist ascended the hall. The friend placed the medicine bag and staff on the steps. The Taoist said, "You have the appearance of a sage, hence you have arrived here." Opening the bag, taking out a ladle, and giving a pill to swallow, he said, "If you are hungry or thirsty, you can chew a little bit of cypress leaves and nuts." The friend sincerely prayed to stay in the Taoist hall to serve as a laborer. The Taoist said, "You should return home, set up a separate room, and deeply contemplate the profound teachings. I will come to welcome you on the eighth day of the ninth month." The friend had not finished bowing when the black dog started barking, so they went out to avoid it. The previous encounter seemed lost, silent and without effect, as if in a dream. After a moment, they saw a woodcutter, who said it was the path to the Immortal Valley Temple in Qingcheng Mountain.

A Man Named Jun You Jun You returned home without thoughts of hunger or thirst, then moved to a separate room and neglected household affairs. He often burned *bì* (柏) seeds and leaves, sitting quietly without engaging in any activity, abstaining from eating and drinking, and occasionally chewing a few *bì* (柏) seeds. Outside the door was a *bì* (柏) tree, and beneath it was a large stone. He regularly wove straw sandals and rested on the stone. On the night of the seventh day of the ninth month, the valley was bathed in clear moonlight and a cool breeze. Jun You seemed to be waiting for something in front of and behind his dwelling. At dawn, the sky was filled with colorful clouds. Jun You looked up into the sky, a gentle wind suddenly arose, and colorful mist rose. His wife and children cried out. He crossed great ravines and mountains, weeping and looking back, until he disappeared from sight. The entire village witnessed the event. During this time, the local officials, Right Censor Li Gong Zhun and Vice-Prefect Zhang Gong Wei, interpreted the event as a sign of evil or deceit. They imprisoned Jun You's wife and children and sent officials and civilians to search for his whereabouts. The villagers were distressed by the officials' actions and burned incense, making a collective appeal: "If Jun You has achieved enlightenment, we beg you to return and not let the villagers suffer unjustly."

One day, a friend of the master appeared before the prefectural office to request an audience. The deputy prefect, Zhang Gong, angrily scolded him and said, "If you were truly an immortal, you would have already departed; how could you return, clearly making you a demon." He was about to impose punishment and detention, but the friend merely lowered his head and sat silently, refusing to eat or drink. A clerk privately asked him, "How can you avoid punishment?" The friend replied, "A new ruler is about to ascend the throne; why worry about not being pardoned?" His words were calm and composed, yet no one understood them. In early December, when Emperor Taizong ascended the throne and issued a general pardon, it was then that everyone realized the proof of the new ruler's ascension. The friend returned home and entered his old rooms, where true immortals occasionally descended, their radiance illuminating the sky as they climbed onto beds and couches, laughing and talking all night. His wife and children, listening to it, could not understand. On the third day of the third month in the first year of the Taiping Xingguo era, he ascended into the sky and slowly departed from a stone beneath a cypress tree. His wife and children watched him until he was in the heavens, hearing only music and fragrant winds all day long. The local prefecture reported the matter to the authorities, who bestowed favors on his wife and children with grain and silk. The prison official Zhang Hanqiu, witnessing these events, therefore abandoned his wife and children to wander through famous mountains, and he is still alive to this day.

**The Practitioner of Yōng Dào** Yōng Dào Zhě, whose name was Fazhi, was a native of Yuánhé Village, Feiwu County, in the East River Region. Though he was simple and rustic in appearance, his nature yearned for purity and emptiness. He often worshipped a stone statue of Laozi and recited the *Tianpeng* incantation from the *Zhenzhong Jing* (Scripture in the Pillow). One night, he dreamed of a Daoist who said to him, "Yōng Fazhi, I will ask for three thousand strings of cash from you." Fazhi, declining on the grounds of his poverty, was then shown by the Daoist a palm broom placed before the stone statue, and told, "Whenever there is a sick person, simply sweep them with this broom and they will be cured." After the Daoist spoke these words, Fazhi awoke. From then on, whenever villagers fell ill, they would have the broom swept over them, and they would be instantly healed. Word spread among the people, and soon those seeking medical help flocked in great numbers. At that time, the Qingyang Palace, located in the southwest of the county city, was the very place where Laozi was said to have been born. During the Xianping era, it was destroyed by war and fire, but only the Yuanyang Twin Platforms, the site of Laozi’s birth, remained. Now, the site was in ruins, overgrown with tall grass.

In the year of the Earth Rooster, the local governor, a scholar-official named Ren Gong, requested the restoration of the old site. Every night in the east of the hall, a mysterious sound of a bell could be heard, but the cause was unknown. While digging a pond, a bronze bell was discovered, and when struck, its sound could be heard for more than thirty miles. Scholars and commoners flocked to visit and observe, and the event lasted from spring through summer. The official of the law saw a little boy with a hunched back walking at the palace gate. The boy swept with a broom, straightened his waist, and left. The crowd was so thick that people were shoulder to shoulder, and the official of the law regarded him as a deity. At that time, workers in the palace who had pain in their waist, legs, or arms were all cured by the boy's sweeping. Thus, it spread far and wide that the person named Yong Dao could cure the blind to see and the lame to walk. Patients gathered like clouds, and those who brought gold to wait at the gate for a long time did not get swept. The money and silk he received were all sent to the construction site. After more than a hundred days, because he was attracted to a woman, he secretly left and did not return, and patients gradually stopped coming. By that winter of the same year, when he came again to sweep, there was no response, and he felt ashamed and fled. Thus, he questioned the person in charge of the construction records, and the money was only about three thousand strings of cash, exactly matching the number from a dream.

Chapter Two Wandering Scholar There was a wandering scholar whose name and hometown are unknown. He often carried a bamboo staff and a basket, leading a spotted dog, traveling between Qiong and Bo, making the collection of herbs his occupation. He often stayed in abandoned temples and ruined monasteries, not avoiding snow, frost, wind, or rain. He leisurely strolled through marketplaces, and when people asked about the way of cultivation, he remained silent without responding. Those who were interested in him often invited him to drink. Over several years, his appearance and clothing never changed. In the spring of the Wuxu year of Tianxi, he said he had traveled to Mount Qingcheng, and upon returning, the magistrate of Linqiong, Shi Zhongran, who had a strong interest in Taoist arts, wanted to see him. He summoned the scholar and said, "Do you drink?" The scholar replied, "I have a little medicine. If you can take it, I will also drink." Shi Zhongran took the medicine, and they each drank several cups. After a while, they talked and the scholar said, "We, the wild people, have hearts close to the clouds and cranes. Having lived in the city for a long time, we greatly miss our hometown and have something important to entrust you with." He then left, heading towards the old post road. Shi Zhongran took the medicine and gradually felt light and at ease, and he sent someone to find the scholar. On April 27th, the scholar went alone, carrying a staff and a backpack, to a temple called Guoning, one mile along the stream. He sat at the temple gate. A passerby asked him, "It is getting late, why are you sitting here for so long?" The scholar replied, "I have a teacher here." As night fell, he suddenly died at the temple gate. The local elders informed the official, and he was temporarily buried on the side of the road.

In the sixth month, Master Zhao heard and said, "Is this what was meant by the previous statement about missing one's hometown for a long time?" He sent the official Zhao Xiu to burn it. When the body was exhumed, its appearance was still lifelike, with hands and feet soft as if in deep sleep. Soon, clear water gushed out from under the body, lifting it and causing it to rise, as if for a bath. The villagers gathered to watch, some dressing the body and others setting up wine and food for a ritual. Master Zhao said, "I have heard that immortals do not die. If they do die, it is through '尸解.' Is this person truly undergoing such a transformation? Though the body has shed its form, the spirit has not yet departed far. I myself stopped my salary to bury him, and also to honor this extraordinary person. Could this be what was meant by the previous statement about a teacher being present here?" He had also read the *Dengzhen Yinde* (Secrets of Ascending to the True), which speaks of the ways of immortality: those who ascend to heaven and tread clouds, those who travel among the Five Great Mountains, those who eat elixirs to achieve immortality, and those who undergo '尸解' to become immortals. Suddenly, there is a brief journey to the dark region (太阴), where the Great One (太一) guards the body, the three souls nurture the bones, and the seven spirits protect the flesh. Even if the primordial spirit is locked away in iron and stone, eventually, after repeated transformations, the body will break through the rock and fly into the void to become an immortal. Those who have attained the Dao can enter fire without being scorched, enter water without being wet, walk on empty space as if on solid ground, and touch solid ground as if it were empty. Even the depth of the nine regions, the breadth of the great seas, the distance of the eight corners of the universe, and the vastness of all directions, can be reached instantly, so why should they be bound or obstructed? This is because the form is united with the Dao, which is everywhere. The Dao exists in the finest details and in all things. Now, this is recorded in full, as a basis for the teachings of Master Zhang.

Fellow Master Cui Fellow Master Cui, whose name was Wuyi, came from the Jiang and Wu regions after Wang Shouyi had taken control of Shu. He feigned deafness and dumbness. Truly, he was a man of the Way. He could discern auspicious and ominous signs by observing people's handwriting, and he could perceive hidden matters, such as the whereabouts of fugitives, secrets buried in the mountains, the timing of life and death, and the safety of relatives living thousands of miles away, without ever failing in his predictions. At that time, both court officials and commoners revered him as if he were a deity. The Taoist Master Tang Dongqing of Longxing Temple ordered a young boy to carry a container of radishes to Master Du Guangting, the Celestial Master. When Tang encountered Fellow Master Cui sitting at the temple gate, he asked him to play the game of "guessing the hidden object." Fellow Master Cui instructed the boy to draw a character on the ground, and the boy drew the character. Fellow Master Cui said, "It is a radish." The boy returned with the radishes and picked up a broken comb, placing it in the container. He then asked Fellow Master Cui to play the game again. Fellow Master Cui said, "Draw a character on the ground." The boy pointed to the character he had drawn, and Fellow Master Cui said, "It is a comb."

Dong Qing was surprised that the child came late, and the child fully answered with Cui's response to the divination. Dong Qing had long known that Cui had the Dao, so he ordered the child to hold an empty fist and point to this character. Cui said, "It's just an empty fist." Dong Qing personally went to see Cui and said, "Three times, the character was divined and each time it was different. Only someone with the Dao could achieve this." Cui replied, "It was all the child who spoke first; I, the old man, don't know it. This character resembles a radish, also a comb, also an empty fist. What is there to know about the Dao?" Cui interpreted characters and objects in a similar manner. The First Sovereign of the Later Shu Dynasty was enfeoffed as King of Shu in the year Jiazi of Tianfu. After Bo Sheng, the inner city was expanded. In the southwest, the Jade Bureau was gathered, and the Five Phoenix Tower gates were built. The five gates with their battlements were grand, adorned with gold and jade, reaching the utmost in beauty. They shone brightly over the main streets, and were named the Virtuous Talent Pavilion, a marvel of the age. The Jade Bureau's revered images were moved to Longxing Temple, and halls and broad storerooms were established on its site. At that time, Master Du visited Cui and said, "Is there any evidence that the current ruler has been moved by divine transformation?" Cui sighed for a long time and said, "It is because the heir apparent will cause trouble."

In the year of Jiashen, Crown Prince Yuan Ying, who was falsely accused of rebellion, was soon executed. Later, Daoist Master Du asked Cui, saying: "Did the wise men foresee this?" Cui replied: "This was an inevitable outcome of stirring up chaos. How could the wise men have foreseen it?" Daoist Master Du then asked: "What will ultimately happen with this transformation?" Cui said: "The situation must return to its original state, and only the Wang family can do it." The first ruler died, and the younger ruler succeeded to the throne. The following year, another miraculous transformation occurred, which was seen as evidence of the Wang family's role in restoring the situation. In the year Jia Yin of the reign of Emperor Zhenzong of the Song Dynasty, Governor and Counselor-in-Adviser Ling Gong Cai submitted a request to move the shrine of the first Wang ruler. The materials from the shrine were used to renovate the transformation, and the construction was extremely thorough, with grand and splendid towers and halls. The carpentry and construction work was not yet completed when, in the Jade Pavilion Cave, a five-colored cloud appeared. Over a thousand people watched the event, and after some time, the crowd dispersed. Soon after, a drawing of the event was presented, and an imperial edict was issued praising it. This was precisely the evidence of the Wang family's role in restoring the situation that Cui had spoken of, how far-reaching it was!

休复 once read the *Immortal Transmissions Supplement*, which states that each of the Twenty-Four Transformations has a great cave, some deep and wide for a thousand or five hundred li. Within these caves are the sun and moon's flying essences, known as the roots of hidden celestial bodies, illuminating the cave entrances and appearing no different from the world below. There are immortal kings, officials, and ministers who serve as aides, much like the officials of the world. All those who have attained the Dao, accumulated merit, transferred their spirits, and returned to life reside within these caves, serving as the common people. Every year on the Three Yuan and Eight Festivals, various heavenly immortals descend into the caves to observe the principles of good and evil in human affairs, the cycles of birth, death, rise, and fall, as well as floods, droughts, and storms. These phenomena are all related to the cave temples and the officials who manage the temples of dragon deities, all of which are overseen by the cave temples. Beyond the Twenty-Four Transformations, there are caves such as Qingcheng, Emei, Yide, Cimo, Fanyang, and Baozhong, which are not included in the Ten Great Caves or the Thirty-Six Caves. The officials of the cave temples are also like the administrative divisions of the human world. What heaven possesses, who can abolish it? To go against heaven will certainly bring great misfortune, and the disaster of disorder among the sons will surely befall you. —Fan the Scholar

Fan Dezhao, a hermit from Shu, was known for his works *Treatise on Universal Principles*, *Treatise on Truth and the Correction of Errors*, and *Treatise on the Elixir of Gold*. He was frequently summoned into the palace by the ruler of Later Shu to discuss his teachings, which pleased the ruler, and he was treated with great respect. Fan Dezhao often spoke of practical matters and moral lessons, emphasizing the importance of self-reflection and caution. During the Ghost Festival, it was customary in Shu for grains to grow abundantly, known as "pot grass," which were used for offerings to the dead. Initially, people were strictly prohibited from touching the grass, believing it was protected by thunder. After the festival, the grass was discarded into the fields. Fan Dezhao sighed and said, "How could one know that the sages followed the clarity of heaven, producing the six qi, and used the five elements based on the nature of the earth? They made plows from wood and hoes from wood, teaching the world the benefits of farming and planting grains to nourish people. Yet, people do not understand the benevolence of heaven and earth, and so lightly discard grains. It is fitting that the gods do not protect them, and they claim to suffer misfortune. How sad!" Li Dezhao, another hermit, was known for his extensive knowledge. He taught the Five Classics and skillfully guided and taught his students, answering their questions openly. For over forty years, he supported himself by teaching. When he taught the *Spring and Autumn Annals*, he often said, "Confucius saw the decline of the Zhou dynasty and the strength of the feudal lords. Though there were rituals, they could not be upheld, and though there were rewards and punishments, they could not be enforced. Confucius therefore edited the annals of Lu, adhering to the system of the Duke of Zhou and clarifying the laws for the future, using praise and censure in place of rewards and punishments to encourage the virtuous and warn the wicked." Zuo Qiuming, the historian of the state of Lu, studied the classics under Confucius and feared that his seventy disciples might develop divergent interpretations, losing the true meaning. Thus, he collected records from various states and wrote the *Zuo Zhuan*.

In the *Zuo Zhuan*, it sometimes begins with the *Spring and Autumn Annals* to start a narrative, and at other times ends with the *Spring and Autumn Annals* to conclude an argument. It also sometimes relies on the *Spring and Autumn Annals* to argue a point, and at other times deviates from the *Spring and Autumn Annals* to reconcile differences. It compiles a wide range of records and statements to form a complete work. Du Zhengnan did not consider that the *Zuo Zhuan* should be treated as equal to the *Five Classics* (including the *Book of Poetry*, *Book of Documents*, *Book of Changes*, *Book of Rites*, and *Spring and Autumn Annals*), and that Zuo Qiuming's commentary should be placed on the same level as the works of Sima Qian and Ban Gu. How can one combine the dates from the *Spring and Autumn Annals* with those from the *Zuo Zhuan*? This practice allows scholars without proper training to freely interpret the texts, focusing only on the phonetic and rhythmic aspects without understanding the ancient principles. As a result, the teachings of Confucius and Mencius and the writings of Sima Qian and Ban Gu cannot be understood without the *Zuo Zhuan*. However, the principles of governance and the patterns of rise and fall cannot be understood through such an approach. Several contemporary scholars have cited the *Zuo Zhuan* as the source of the *Spring and Autumn Annals*, but these citations are not recorded here. If the *Spring and Autumn Annals* were treated as a separate text and the *Zuo Zhuan* as a commentary, it would prevent confusion among future generations.

The Magistrate Su In the southwest corner of the secondary city wall of Shu, there was a Taoist priest who operated a divination stall, predicting the good or bad fortune in a person's life with the certainty of seeing it with one's own eyes. During the Guangzheng era of Shu, two scholars, Su Xie and Du Xiyan, visited him together. The Taoist priest told Su: "You will certainly become famous next year." Su did not fully believe him, but the Taoist priest said: "It is already fixed, and you will also have a noble son." At the time, Du's wife was pregnant and close to giving birth, so he began to believe the prediction. The Taoist priest then said to Du: "Why is it so late for you to succeed?" Du was displeased and thought the prediction was false, so he left angrily. The next spring, Su passed the imperial examinations under the auspices of the Chancellor Jia, and Du indeed did not succeed. Su had a son at the banquet in the Apricot Garden, named Yi Jian. He later became a Secretary of the Ministry of Rites and Chief Advisor to the Emperor.

Resolving the uncertainty in the term "解南宫" requires careful consideration of the context and the likely meaning based on the surrounding text. Here's the reasoning: 1. **Contextual Analysis**: The term "解南宫" appears in the sentence "杜始得成都解南宫奏名登第，授常州军事推官，不禄." "解" could be interpreted as "to resolve" or "to handle," possibly indicating a role in managing or overseeing the examination process. 3. **Plausible Interpretations**: - **解南宫 as a Title**: It could be an official title, possibly related to the administration of the imperial examination. - **解南宫 as a Place**: It could refer to a specific location within the imperial palace where the examination was held or managed. 4. **Most Likely Reading**: Given the context, the most plausible interpretation is that "解南宫" is an official title related to the imperial examination system, possibly indicating a role in managing or overseeing the examination process. **Final Translation**: Upon realizing the Taoist's words, Du returned to visit him and asked: "Although it is said that my official rank is achieved late, I have not yet known the year my official career began or where it will end." The Taoist said: "Scholar, strive and you will surely achieve great fame. However, the matter is slightly different, and I cannot explain it." Du pleaded with him, and the Taoist said: "The day you achieve success will be under the seat of Su Xian's eldest son." Du said: "If I preserve these words, can I abstain from blessings and official rank?" The Taoist replied: "From now on, it cannot be known." That year, Su was appointed as the Judicial Officer of Pengzhou, then changed to the Military Censor of Luchou. Zhang Hai Shang Zhang Guang, a juren from Pseudo-Shu, styled Hai Shang, in the year Bing Xu of the Yongxi era, traveled to Jia Prefecture to visit the magistrate of Pingqiang. His boat moored at night below Pingqiang Gai, when he suddenly dreamed of two figures with dignified and solemn appearances, clad in radiant white robes, who knelt before him and begged for help. When Zhang Guang awoke, he heard only the unceasing sound of jumping and leaping below the boat’s platform. Upon looking, he saw two carp. Zhang Guang, whose nature was hasty and impatient, could not tolerate others, and was angered that the fish had disturbed his sleep. He climbed onto the boat’s platform, took the fish, and cast them into the river. Soon after, he went to sleep again, and dreamed once more of the two white-robed figures, who now held several heads of garlic and earnestly thanked him before departing. It was only at dawn that he realized the dreamer had been the fish. Upon arriving at Pingqiang, he recounted the dream to the magistrate of Pingqiang. The magistrate said, “Your dream is a sign of good fortune. The garlic the fish gave you is a symbol of calculation; your lifespan shall be extended.” In his later years, Zhang Guang composed three volumes of *Hou Yin Shu*, which also recorded the story of his dream with the fish. He lived to be seventy-eight and then passed away.

### The Daoist Abbot of Lingyang Supreme Dao Temple, Fei Yuguì The Daoist abbot of Lingyang Supreme Dao Temple, Fei Yuguì, courtesy name Tiansi, was excellent in literature and scholarship, praised by his contemporaries. He once attempted to take the imperial examination in the state of Shu, where his name was Xuan. He had a dream in which he saw himself wearing splendid robes inside a well. Upon waking, he was delighted and said, "Passing the examination, wearing splendid robes, and returning to my hometown." It is said that those who take the examination often have strange dreams, and Fei Yuguì mentioned his dream to Su. Su replied, "It is not a good dream. Wearing splendid robes in a well is a sign that your talent has not yet been recognized." Fei Yuguì was not pleased and indeed failed the examination the following spring, returning to his hometown. He then told Su, "Life is like a candle flame, lasting only a hundred years. One can only find joy in nurturing the spirit and cultivating aspirations,寄托情感于诗酒，不能再成为琐碎的儒生，确实有云栖之志。" Su said, "Worldly honors are temporary, life is like a guest, and only ascending to true nature and following the Way can be pursued at a later time."

I have a foolish child, and I wish to teach him. He is now a Councillor and Attendant. By the following year, the imperial court launched a campaign against Shu. Su Shangjing served in various positions and was granted the title of Judicial Officer of Kaifeng during the Tianping Xingguo period, where he died in office. I once read medical texts which stated that when a person's yin energy is abundant, they dream frequently, and when yang energy is strong, they dream rarely. Even if they do dream, they are not remembered. The blind do not dream, and the foolish dream seldom. Therefore, servants and laborers may go a hundred nights without a dream, showing that dreaming is a learned behavior. It is not exclusive to sages either. Recently, a scholar was able to interpret dreams. He composed a dream for interpretation, and the predictions came true. Later, he confessed, "I actually had no dream; the dream I composed was fabricated by me, just to test you, and the predictions were all correct. Why is that?" The dream interpreter replied, "The meaning is expressed in words, and the predictions follow." How much more so for dreams of writing or nature? Thus, it is known that dreams cannot be understood by a single interpretation.

Mr. Feng, the Recluse Mr. Feng, whose given name was Huigu and style name Duchun, was from Suining. He possessed insight into human relations and was skilled in discussing mountains, rivers, and geography. During the reign of Emperor Taizong in the太平兴国 period, he admired flowers in front of Niu Xin Mountain at the three paths of Qingcheng Mountain and chose to settle down behind the mountain, building a large pavilion of three rooms and resting within it. In his daily discussions, he spoke of the principles of alchemy, engaging in breathing exercises and guiding practices. He extensively collected formulas, spells, songs, diagrams, alchemical texts, and Daoist scriptures, examining and studying them without exception. Whenever he encountered travelers who took elixirs, sought immortality in his room, gained various skills, mastered various alchemical substances, boasted of their longevity, claimed to be hundreds of years old, or frequently interacted with immortals, those who wished to pass on knowledge sought alchemical texts, and those who taught exchanged gold and silk as payment. He treated them all with close attention and respect.

Even though their skills are not impressive, they should all be treated with courtesy. During the reign of Emperor Zhenzong of Song, a wealthy family in Chengdu sought to bury their father. They visited all those skilled in geomancy to select a mountain and determine a burial site, and after several years, they finally found one. They then had Feng inspect the site, and Feng said, "The hills wind around, the mountains are high and long, and the water flows clearly. This is very extraordinary." The owner of the site said, "Since the burial, our family's wealth has been depleted, and our population has dwindled. How can such an extraordinary place lead to such misfortune?" The geomancer replied, "I will explain it briefly. Among all things, humans are the most intelligent, receiving a mandate from heaven that is different from other things, and there are different social statuses, each occupying its appropriate place. Just as birds have their nests and animals have their dens, there is no mutual competition. This mountain is suitable for burying princes and dukes; how could ordinary people live here? Therefore, the deceased cannot rest peacefully, and the living cannot find peace." He then quoted the *Yijing* (Book of Changes), saying, "Carrying a burden and riding in a carriage invites thieves; even a lowly person using the tools of a noble will bring misfortune. Is this what it means?"

Li Si Lang Li Si Lang, whose name was Xuan and courtesy name Tingyi, was originally a Persian national. He followed Emperor Xizong into Sichuan during the An Lushan Rebellion and was granted the rank of Shuifu Shuai. His elder brother, Xun, was known for his poetry and participated in the imperial examinations. Li Si Lang was graceful in his manners and had a virtuous character. He made a living by selling incense and medicinal herbs, was skilled at playing chess, and favored self-cultivation, dedicating himself to the Taoist practice of taking elixirs to prolong life. In his old age, the expenses of his alchemical furnaces left him with no surplus wealth, only Taoist texts and medicinal pouches. He once obtained a letter from Mr. Erzhu and Mr. Nanliu of Qingcheng, which discussed the method of refining "autumn stone" from Huainan. He would burn incense to scent it, and there was a peach pit cup about an inch in circumference, brightly colored and patterned, truly the fruit of the mythical peach tree of immortality. He eventually consumed it in his later years. In the first year of the Yongxi era, he traveled to Qingcheng Mountain and found a stone in the stream below Sixshi Rock, shaped like a goose egg, black and smooth. He often played with it along with fellow Taoists. One day, it accidentally fell to the ground and broke into several pieces. Inside was hollow, capable of holding about a dou of something, with all sides carved with dragon, phoenix, cloud, and grass patterns, intricately detailed and very unusual, likely not made by human hands. Some said it was an object played with by immortals.

**Volume Three** **The Sand Sifter** In the large eastern market of the Former Shu, there was an institution called the "Nursing House for the Poor and Sick," where beggars, the poor, and the sick could stay. Among them was a person who carried a basket with an iron handle and sifted through the mud and sand in the streets and ditches. He often found small pieces of copper, iron, and various other items, which he used for sustenance. People called him the Sand Sifter. In the year of Xinyou, there was a man who lived incognito among the sand sifters, whose origins and name were unknown. He usually wore an old hat and carried a bamboo basket with an iron handle, often sitting or lying in quiet and secluded places in temples. A scholar-official named Wen Gu, who had failed the imperial examinations, went to the Saint Xing Temple to visit a friend. There, he saw the Sand Sifter sitting on the Buddha hall, wearing a rough garment. Wen Gu was struck by the man's ancient and stern appearance and his clear and lofty speech. He greeted him with respect. Wen Gu then asked the man to recite some of his newly composed poems, and he was startled. He also recited several of his own compositions, which included poems that criticized the current social and political situation, warned against common vices, or spoke of matters related to immortals and supernatural beings.

The depth of the river is unknown, so he asked the river: Where are you going now? The river replied: I am visiting a temple to meet a familiar monk, seeking a small amount of money for paper and ink, and planning another contribution. The person reached into his inner garment and pulled out a cloth bag containing several small silver ingots strung together with hemp rope. He then untied one of the ingots and gave it to the river, put on a hat, took the items he was carrying, bowed respectfully, and left the temple. Later, the river was honored by Wang Zhaoyuan, a high-ranking official, at a banquet. During the conversation, they spoke of the story of encountering Tao Sha Zi, and recited his poem: "In the ninefold city, a person of high status; Among the five ranks of nobility, a nobleman respected beyond the palace gates. How could it compare to a simple scholar wandering like a cloud and water? Not hanging one's name in the heavens and earth." Wang said: There is such a strange person! The story was then reported to the ruler of Shu, who ordered the inner garden officials to search for him in the various streets and alleys. At that time, there was a man named Yuwen living on Guoqing Temple Street in the eastern market. His house had a large camphor tree, and Tao Sha Zi was resting in the shade of the tree. Yuwen was deeply interested in the Daoist path and noticed that the man had a unique appearance. He invited him into the hall and asked about his skills and pursuits. Tao replied: I love reading and drinking wine, and my words are not conventional.

They drank several cups together and made an agreement to meet again. After ten days, 淘沙子 sometimes came to his door, leaving a broken hat and other items with the door servant, and ordered the servant to report to the owner. The servant angrily shouted at him, saying, "How could the master see such poor children?" 宇文 heard this and immediately went out to greet him, apologizing humbly, "I have been longing for you for a long time, why did you come so late?" They immediately continued drinking and became merry. 宇文 asked, "Can one attain immortality, and can one seek the ultimate Dao?" 淘沙子 replied, "Gaining it is in the heart, and losing it is also in the heart." 宇文 said, "Several years ago, I met someone who taught me to practice breath control, but I did not achieve the desired effect. I have abandoned it for a long time." 淘沙子 said, "Practicing the Dao as at the beginning, one achieves the Dao with abundance. All are initially diligent but become lazy in the middle, and the previous efforts will be abandoned." 宇文 asked, "Are there yellow and white (metals) in the world? Do they exist, and do you like them?" 宇文 replied, "Although I have not paid attention to them, how dare I say they do not exist, and how dare I say I do not like them."

Tao Shaizi asked for ten coins, took a pill from his belt, soaked and applied it with vinegar, and then burned it into silver. This is the art of immortals, and it should not be heavily slandered, but it is rarely encountered. Those who claim to possess such a skill are all lying. He hastily bid farewell and left. The next day at dawn, he knocked on the door, bringing a new handkerchief wrapped around something and saying, "Tao Shaizi has sent this to the master." Yuwen opened and looked at it, and it was a single hair from a bun, the cause of which was unfathomable. By the time the sun was high, the door servant did not come, and he was ordered to summon him. The servant said, "This morning at the fifth watch of the night, while sleeping, someone cut off the hair from the bun and took it away." The ruler of Shu heard of this and inquired of Yuwen. Yuwen found at the hospital for the sick and said, "This morning he went out and did not return." From this point on, there was no further trace of him. A Daoist Monk Named Zhang In the eastern suburbs of the pseudo-Shu kingdom, there stood a pagoda named Miaoyuan. Inside the pagoda was a monk named Xingqin, whose secular surname was Zhang. People called him a Daoist because of his proficiency in cultivation. In his early years, he traveled south, and in his middle age, he settled down. He had thick, white eyebrows and hair, an ancient appearance, and a weak physique. He lived in several thatched houses, with only a rope bed and a wooden coffin. He did not participate in communal meals, sitting on the bed during the day and sleeping in the coffin at night. He never changed his clothes. When asked questions, he remained silent and did not respond. People all admired his noble character. If given clothes, he would give them to the poor. He accepted rice, flour, salt, and dairy products, storing them in a large bottle that was always full. He took a small amount and ate it over three years, resting calmly and naturally. He did not deceive the common people; his purity and nobility were like this. At the age of eighty, before his death, he gathered firewood and grass in the courtyard. Later, he told his disciples: "I will leave in a few days. Hope to place the wooden coffin on the firewood and grass and burn it. The old monk will be fortunate." On the appointed day, following his instructions, several dozen relics were obtained from the ashes, and he was buried in the pagoda.

At that time, there was a revered elder named Cici, a master of the Zen school, who wrote on the wall of Zhang Dao's room in the Miaoyuan Pagoda Temple: In Chengdu, there lived a hermit named Zhang Dao, who had resided in the countryside for fifty years. He maintained a simple and unpretentious lifestyle, never acting insincerely or superficially in response to others. He had extensively studied and traveled throughout the southern regions of the Zen tradition and completed his journeys through the forests (monasteries) of the western Sichuan region. He possessed a deep understanding of the Buddhist scriptures as a means to resolve entanglements and did not rigidly adhere to the distinctions between the three vehicles of the Buddha's teachings. Zhang Dao lived near a stream and in a secluded hermitage, wearing clothes made of grass and living in a thatched hut. He had no possessions or material needs, avoiding being bothered by others asking for things. He woke up early for meals and napped at noon, finding contentment and joy in this routine. He did not thank donors for their offerings, valuing his spiritual worth over material wealth. He believed that his wisdom was priceless and could not be quantified. Zhang Dao had an ancient and clear appearance, beyond the ability to be captured in a painting. His nature was as free and natural as a crane and clouds, and he was completely fearless of life and death. Even in the face of great calamities, he remained unharmed, and even the hidden spirits and dragons were amazed by his state. Zhang Dao did not speak about Zen or engage in conversation, as he found it difficult to guide or transform people. Most people rushed after fame and profit, aimlessly and hurriedly, lacking true wisdom. He clearly told people to stop their minds from wandering, but despite his efforts, some people still dismissed the teachings as trivial. Zhang Dao did not gather disciples and lived a very free and detached life, not forming a community like the White Lotus Society established by Far Gong. His heart was like the moon in a clear autumn pond, why should he seek fame and reputation throughout the world?

**Great Enlightenment Zen Master** The Zen master's name was Cici, his courtesy name Fati, and his family name Liu. He traveled southward at the end of the Western Shu dynasty, and returned to reside at the beginning of the Later Shu dynasty. The Great Enlightenment Zen Master was quick-tempered and quick in speech, answering questions with fluid ease. Someone asked: "In the *Lotus Sutra*, which part is the *wonderful dharma*?" The master struck his hand and said: "I will teach you to have a collapsed nose. Why is it like this?" The person replied: "Because I slandered this sutra, and thus I have suffered this fate." Li Jiong, a high official of the Later Shu court, once asked the master questions about the Dharma, and treated him with great honor. The master composed *A Guide for Zen Practitioners*, *The Genealogy of Zen Patriarchs*, and over three hundred verses, songs, and hymns expounding the Dharma, which he titled *The Ultimate Dharma of the Zen School*, and they were circulated throughout the world. **Master Zhang Pingyun** Master Zhang, whose name was Qiao and courtesy name Pingyun, studied the teachings of the Buddha, and was called by people "Master Zhang, the Lay Practitioner." At one time, a fellow lay practitioner named Gou asked: "One who is not bound by birth and death, may the teacher point it out directly?" He replied: "Not relying on the sun and moon to illuminate, day and night it is clear on its own." The questioner then asked: "When the light of the non-pointed (ineffable) does not hinder the mountains and rivers, even in the hundredfold coming and going, how is this?" He answered: "The red tail sways in three-foot waves, the true dragon passes through stone, leaving no trace." He once composed *Notes on Inquiry into the Profound*, *The Collection of the Mysterious Pearl*, and over a hundred poems, verses, and songs, in which he wrote: "A traveler in coarse robes comes to ask about my family tradition. I say that everywhere is clear and open. In an instant, all nations are reached, yet on the way I have never met anyone." When he passed away, three days later, his mouth exhaled a fragrant breath that filled the room with a subtle aroma. One of his disciples said: "Master Zhang often said that the Zen school takes the eyes as the priority, and does not take sleeping posture as a matter. Why is it that Master Zhang is like this today?" After he spoke, the fragrance ceased.

The Lay Practitioner Wang The lay practitioner Wang Yu devoted himself to Zen studies for over forty years, having thoroughly visited all the Zen monasteries in the Three Shus. Scholars and students debated with him, and his words could not escape his precise judgment. In his later years, he fell ill and told his fellow practitioners: "I will go forth and teach on a certain day." On that day, his younger uncle, who was a monk, said to him: "I will recite the name of Amitabha Buddha ten times for you." Wang Yu replied: "To transcend the formless, to be unobstructed in all directions, to reach the state of no-mind—this has not yet been achieved, let alone the presence of thoughts." With these words, he passed away suddenly. The Lay Practitioner Gou The lay practitioner Gou, whose name was Lingxuan, was from Shu Prefecture. He followed the lineage of Zhang Pingyun, who engaged in dialogues with students, responding to their questions with apt answers. He wrote works such as the *Huolian Collection*, *Wuxiang Baoshan Treatise*, *Fayin Transmission*, and over a hundred miscellaneous writings on the Way. He composed a poem as a tribute to the stupa of Vatukong Master, saying: "The vast emptiness, countless eons reduced to dust; his steps are profound, solitary, and transcendent. Coming from Japan to seek the other shore, passing through the confused crossing in the mountains and forests. The flowing Dharma milk, who can be excluded from it? Clearly teaching that I am closest to it. After one hundred sixty-three years, only then was his entire body buried in this stupa." Vatukong Master, whose name was Nengguang, was a Japanese national. He was a disciple of Master Dongshan Wuben and entered Sichuan in the early years of Tianfu. The false Yongtai Military Governor Lu Qianyi donated his residence at Biji Lane as a Zen monastery for him to reside in. He passed away at the end of the Longxing era of the Later Shu, at the age of one hundred sixty-three, hence the mention of this verse.

Tang Qiu, a man of Qingcheng County in Shu Prefecture during the late Tang Dynasty, was known as Tang Qiu of the Flavorful Mountains. He was of an extremely sincere and honest nature, deeply devoted to the elegant way of life, living a free-spirited and unrestrained existence, almost like a recluse. Every day, he would ride a green ox into town and return home in a state of intoxication by dusk. He did not associate with those who were not of his kind, and whenever he had something to say, he would compose poetry, rolling up the drafts into small balls and placing them in a large gourd. For over twenty years, no one knew how many poems he had written, and he also stopped composing poetry. His poems for gifts and farewells were widely known. In his later years, due to illness, he threw the gourd into the river, saying: "If these writings do not sink into the water, future people who find them will understand my hard work." The gourd drifted to the mouth of the Xinqu River, where those who recognized it said: "This is the poetry gourd of Tang Mountain Man." When they retrieved it, it was already damaged by the river, and only about one-third survived. About thirty poems circulated in the world. He dedicated a poem to the hermit Zheng at his retreat: "I heard that it is the most serene and expansive; when I arrived, my worries were gone. A few drops of rain from the stone spring, a stream of frosty leaves and wind. His profession is in the mountains, his way of life is in the midst of nothingness."

I drank up a full cup of wine, and the sick man's face also turned red. Giving a gift to a departing person as if speaking to an elder: "Do not know the bitterness of fame and profit, chanting Buddha's name until old age in the Min River. Patching the robe with thousands of pieces, burning incense in a patterned container. Loving the mountains means few worldly affairs, caring for guests means many spiritual thoughts. After the daily bell for fasting, hanging high a net to filter water. Writing a poem about the Fan Xian Temple on Qingcheng Mountain: several miles along the mountain, not tiring of the difficulty, to seek the true secret, asking the Taoist priest. Moss covering the green spots on the fairy bridge, making it slippery, pine trees weaving fragrant branches on the ancient path, making it cold. In the daytime, beside the green fields, hoeing tender jade, at night, opening the red stove to knead new elixir. The sound of the bell has stopped, but the sound of the spring remains, the wind moves the jade flowers, and the moon fills the altar. Giving a gift to a monk: "Once opened half a verse in the snow mountain, when the leaves of the sacred tree are turned, all principles are understood. Wisdom constantly adds the strength of precepts, who can calculate the merit of chanting scriptures. The dawn moon should be difficult to stain, the lone boat at sea relies on the wind. Often saying that the flowers in the courtyard are beautiful, one red branch is one empty branch." Among the grassy wetlands, there are recluses who have achieved their goals, entertaining themselves with scriptures, poetry and wine to soothe the soul, not showing off literary talent, not publicizing their names, how can they not feel ashamed to follow others in a half-hearted way compared to the people of Tangshan?

Preface to the Guests at Orchid Pavilion On the third day of the third month in the late spring of the ninth year of the Yonghe era during the reign of Emperor Mu of Jin, there were forty-one people gathered at the Orchid Pavilion, including Sun Tongcheng of Taiyuan, Sun Chaoxing of Fuchun, Wang Binzhao of Tanghan, Xie An of Chenjun, Xi Tan of Gaoping, Wang Yun of Taiyuan, and the monk Zhide. Among them were the sons of Wang Xizhi: Nengzhi, Weizhi, and Caozhi. They held a ritual gathering for the purification rite (禊), and Wang Xizhi wrote the preface, composing it in a free and spirited manner. His calligraphy was powerful, graceful, and unmatched in strength, consisting of 28 lines and 324 characters. Emperor Taizong of Tang acquired the original manuscript and ordered Zhao Mo, Han Daogeng, Feng Chengsu, and Zhuge Zhen to make copies and distribute them to the crown prince, princes, and close officials. Emperor Taizong was intensely fond of calligraphy and owned 3,600 authentic calligraphy pieces by Wang Xizhi. Each piece was rolled into a scroll measuring 1.2 meters in length, resulting in 150 scrolls.

Emperor Taizong personally inscribed the characters "贞观" on a seal, which he placed at the beginning and end of scrolls. He also selected the children of high-ranking officials who were known for their good character and intelligence, appointing them as students at the Hongwen Academy, and provided them with a model of calligraphy to study. Anyone skilled in calligraphy was also invited to join the academy. As a result, within a few years, calligraphy became widespread throughout the empire. Many people were skilled in writing, but the most treasured of all the calligraphy works of Wang Xizhi was the *Lanting Xu* (Preface to the Poems Composed at the Orchid Pavilion). The emperor often kept this work beside his throne, viewing it morning and evening. In the twenty-third year of the Zhenguan era, when Emperor Taizong fell ill and was on the verge of death, he told Emperor Gaozong: "I wish to ask you for something. You are truly filial, how could you go against my wishes? What do you think?" Emperor Gaozong listened to his command. Emperor Taizong said: "I wish to take the *Lanting Xu* with me. Can you do that for me?" Emperor Gaozong, sobbing and weeping, replied, "Yes." The emperor ordered that on the day of mourning, the *Lanting Xu* be stored in a jade box and sent with the imperial procession to the temple. Today, the copies made by Zhao Mo and others are often collected by enthusiasts. During the reign of the false Wu state, the king of Wu sent the envoy Gao Bi to establish good relations with the court, carrying the national letter to the false Wu state and presenting a stone rubbing of the *Lanting Xu* to the false crown prince. At the time, those who knew about it discussed that this version was a stone rubbing made after Wang Xizhi wrote the preface at the Orchid Pavilion. The false crown prince's calligraphy was highly skilled, and Gao Bi therefore made this presentation. The false crown prince's calligrapher, Mi Daolin, served as a calligrapher to the crown prince and held over a hundred volumes of calligraphy, including works by Wang Xizhi and Wang Xianzhi, the ink traces of ancient famous scholars, and stone rubbings. When the imperial court conquered the false Wu state, all the calligraphy works were taken to Mi Daolin's private collection.

During the Dangde era, there was a man named Wang Qilang, whose name was Wenchang, and he was an old acquaintance of Dao Lin. Through Wang Wenchang's stone edition of the "Preface to the Poems Composed at the Orchid Pavilion," which was originally presented to the crown prince by the envoy Gao Bi from Wu, Dao Lin came to know Wang Wenchang. Wang Wenchang was fond of erudition and collected many famous works of calligraphy from ancient times, including works by Wang Xizhi such as the "Discussion of Lü Yi," "The Yellow Court Classic," and the "Seventeen Postscripts" in cursive script, as well as calligraphy works of famous ministers from the Jin, Wei, Han, and Tang dynasties, and stone editions, all of which were gathered in his home. At that time, those who enjoyed good books would often visit him, including Ma Xizhen, Wang Zhu, Gou Zhongzheng, Zhang Renjian, Huang Jushi, Zhang Dezao, Zhang Wenyi, Shi Dai, Teng Changyou, Shi Ke, Li Dehua, Chen Xizai, and the monk Huaijian Yi Xi. They would read through his collection and forget to be tired all day. At the beginning of the Taiping Xingguo era, Gao Bao Yin, the Crown Prince's Grand Secretary and a descendant of the Gao family from Zhuxia Palace, entered Sichuan as the circuit inspector of the nine prefectures and once visited Wang Wenchang. He obtained a scroll of Zhang Zao's landscape painting and a scroll of Wang Xizhi's calligraphy of the "Preface to the Poems Composed at the Orchid Pavilion," with the words "Chongshan" and "Zhuanghu" inscribed, all of which were copied by Zhao Mo and Zhuge Zhen. The sandalwood scroll and ancient seals were all damaged by smoke and insects, but at that time, he could often enjoy them with his friends. In the year Jia Wu, his collection of paintings and calligraphy was almost entirely burned and looted. Now, after two periods of banditry in Shu, famous books and ancient paintings from various families are rarely seen. Therefore, I have recorded this in detail.

**Chapter Four** **At Home, Tai** In the Former Shu, there was a man named Jia Jutai living in the Fangba area of Meizhou Prefecture. He and his wife were both middle-aged, with only one son who had reached adulthood. Suddenly, the son fell ill, becoming sickly and emaciated for a long time. Despite daily medical treatments, there was no improvement at all. Devoutly, they placed a copy of the "Thousand Gold Prescriptions" on the upper floor of their home, burning incense day and night, looking towards Mount Emei and appealing to the True Person Sun, praying for rescue. After more than ten days, one night, both husband and wife had the same dream: a white-clad old man said, "Your son received less vital energy from your parents at birth. I will now teach you: every morning, you should each blow air into his mouth, and he should open his mouth and swallow it. Do this for three days, and your son will recover." Upon waking, both parents recounted the dream, and they agreed to follow the instructions. They then sincerely followed the teachings from the dream. Initially, the son's bones were not yet strong, but later he was able to eat and walk. Over time, all his illnesses suddenly healed. Later, he became a Taoist after putting on a hat and brown robe, always serving the True Person without tiring.

Zhou Yuanyu, a portrait painter from Chengdu during the false Shu kingdom, was known for his skill in painting portraits. One day, he sought refuge from the heat under the Buddha's tooth pagoda at the Great Compassion Temple. Sometimes he would sigh deeply, and a villager asked him why he was sighing. Zhou Yuanyu replied, "I have been specializing in portrait painting for many years, but my life has been unfortunate. When I am asked to paint portraits, those of wealthy households are not like me, but those of poor households are very similar to me. My mother is old, and I cannot provide for her adequately, which is why I sigh." The villager then asked where Zhou Yuanyu lived. After a while, he said, "I have a small plot of land in Lingchi County, and there is a temple in a neighboring village. The abbot wants to have a portrait painted and has asked me for a long time. Tomorrow morning, come and find me, do not miss this appointment." The next day, a Taoist priest with fair skin and long beard came to ask for a portrait. He said, "Last night, the disciples of the neighboring village mentioned it, so I came to pay respects." Zhou Yuanyu then carefully thought and picked up his brush to finish it immediately. The portrait was not a little different, and the Taoist priest was happy and said, "There is a servant outside, who will give a small gift." When Zhou Yuanyu went out to call him, the Taoist priest had already disappeared. After a while, the people of Shu City all said, "Master Zhu from Lingchi came to Zhou's house to have a portrait painted." People who wanted portraits came in a steady stream. From then on, the income was sufficient to support his mother. Is he not a person of virtue who comes and goes in the world to help the poor and distressed?

丁 Yuanhe 丁 Yuanhe was a person who, from a young age, loved the Dao, did not covet fame or profit, and was aloof and proud, without restraint. On clear days, he would carry a lute, leave the city to drink wine, and walk leisurely among the fields. He often spoke of his grandfather's experiences during the year 930 in Suizhou, when Emperor Meng of Former Shu and Dong Zhong of Eastern Shu were besieging the city. Before the siege, there was a poor man named Song Ziran in the city who often begged in the streets, and the locals could not recognize him. In the midst of the siege, people all starved to death, and Song Ziran also perished from hunger in the marketplace. Those who knew him wrapped him in bamboo mats and buried him under the city walls, awaiting the end of the siege to cremate him. The following year, a subordinate official from Suizhou named Li Yan went to Luchou to handle affairs and returned only after the city was broken. He reported seeing Song Ziran in Luchou and said, "If you return to the state, you must convey my message to five or seven acquaintances; it was very troublesome at that time."

A person responded that he naturally died in the heavy siege, and because he was found at the burial site, only an empty bamboo mat was seen there, with a piece of paper that said: "The heart is the room of the divine spirit, the mouth is the jade pool that produces jade liquid, often use the jade liquid to irrigate the divine room, flow smoothly to nourish all the meridians. When all the meridians are moistened, the branches and leaves turn green, when the branches and leaves are green and the branches are moist, one achieves immortality. The world does not know the elixir of immortality, refining stones and burning elixirs exhausts one's form." Yuanhe therefore learned the Dao and deeply understood its use. Xiu Fu once read the *Taoist Book of Ascending to Truth and Seclusion*, which said that there are eight ways of transformation, the methods of transformation, their ways are secret. Those who mock the Dao only see the mess and beggars in the market, they take it as a mouthful without knowing it. However, once one entrusts oneself to transformation, one must hide one's tracks elsewhere, often changing names, suddenly meeting someone who knows and revealing a few traces, thereby inspiring later generations. If not for those who follow the Dao and seek the unusual, who could gather and understand its hidden and revealed aspects.

**Wang Taomiao (The Temple of the Royal Family)** In the southern part of Chengdu, during the period of the pseudo-Shu state, there was a wine shop run by a woman named Liutiao. At that time, the wine shop was named after the person who operated it. Liutiao was bright and understanding, and many people were familiar with her. She fell seriously ill and was in a vegetative state, waiting for death. One day, a Taoist priest frequently visited the wine shop. Liutiao treated him with great care and respect. Moved by her kindness, the priest gave her several pills and said, "These pills are to repay your debt. Take one every three days by swallowing it with water. After taking all the pills, your illness will be cured." Liutiao followed the instructions. After taking the first pill, she began to recover and could eat again. After taking the second pill, she could walk with a cane. After taking the last pill, she was as healthy as before. There was an official named Wang Daomu at the pseudo-Shu royal temple. He was known as Wang Taomiao, and he was originally from Jintang County in the Han state. He learned about Liutiao's story and asked her for the remaining pills. Wang Daomu put the pills in an iron tea kettle with mercury and heated it. Soon, the mercury turned into gold. He then presented the gold and the pills to the ruler of Shu, claiming that the gold could be used to make utensils to ward off poison, and that it could be used as a plaything to ward off evil spirits. If the pills were taken, they could lead to immortality. The ruler of Shu asked about the method of combining the pills, and Wang Daomu replied, "There is a herb that grows in Sanxue Mountain. I will be sent to govern Jintang to collect the herb." He was appointed as the governor of Jintang. The next year, when the herb did not yield any results, Wang Daomu realized that Liutiao had obtained the pills, and he had her executed. I have often read Daoist texts that say, "There is no one who can achieve immortality or divine arts without practicing Taoism." If someone obtains something through deceit or flattery, they will inevitably face misfortune.

Official Liu Official Liu, whose name was Liu Chan, was handsome in appearance, skilled in conversation, and well-read in historical works. He enjoyed discussing matters of immortals and the supernatural. Without children, he and his wife were both in their fifties or sixties. He served as the deputy magistrate of Zhangdao County in Chengzhou in the false state of Shu. When the legitimate Song Dynasty reclaimed the region, he hid in the Sichuan area selling medicine and changed his name to Baoyi. During the reign of Emperor Taizu (968-976), he built three thatched cottages on the Ghost City Mountain in Qingcheng, planted fruits and vegetables, and made plans to live out his remaining years there. He entered Qingcheng County once or twice a month to sell medicine, buying rice, flour, salt, and cheese to bring back to the mountain. From this, people gradually came to know him. It was rumored that he had the method of turning base metals into gold and silver. One day, three people came at night seeking lodging, bringing their own wine and fruit, and spoke about alchemical matters, finding much common ground. Until late at night, they were laughing and chatting merrily. One of the guests said, "I know that Official Liu has the method of turning base metals into gold and silver. Could you please give us a brief explanation?" Official Liu initially firmly refused, but the guests kept asking persistently.

The official smiled and said, "I have wandered for years following a teacher, and only obtained this method. How could it be lightly spoken of?" The guest said, "We wish to serve in the hidden study as firewood and bellows. Is it acceptable?" The official declined, saying that the teacher's instruction has its time, and promised to pass it on another day before the True Lord. The guest changed expression and said, "It must be passed on tonight. Do not treat it lightly." The official said, "Just now I admired your fellow path, but to be pressured like this." The three of them bared their arms, glared, and stared at him for a long time, saying, "We are not gentlemen, but thieves. If we do not get the method, we will surely harm you." They drew a short knife from their waist, and the official and his wife were terrified, fearing their coercion and threats, and handed over the method, along with the remaining medicine. The three of them took it and bowed and left. The official and his wife went down the mountain in the dark and clear, and did not go again. They then gave the mountain dwelling to the recluse Li Chen. Xiu then taught the Dao to the recluse, thus becoming thoroughly familiar with the matter.

Tian Sunzhi During the reign of the Wang family in the Shu Kingdom, there was an official named Tian Sunzhi. By the time of the Meng dynasty, he had already lived to be a hundred years old, and his wife was also in her nineties. At that time, when the families of court officials held wedding banquets, they would inevitably invite the elderly couple, ostensibly to wish for longevity. By the end of the Shu dynasty, his husband died first, and later, when the Song dynasty conquered the Shu, during the reign of Emperor Taizong in the Taiping Xingguo period, the old woman still survived, living to be a hundred and twenty years old. Her distant descendants lived in the Western Market, making artificial flowers for a living, providing her with minimal support. Occasionally, people of good will would visit her. She was thin and weak in appearance, looking like a child aged twelve or thirteen, with short white hair, and her gaze seemed to regard outsiders as if they were of another species. She was unaffected by cold, heat, wind, or frost. I once read in the *Xiansheng Zhuan* (Biographies of Immortals) that human life can reach a hundred and twenty years, not due to cultivation or practice, but all because of natural endowment. This old woman is an example of that. If one achieves longevity through cultivation and obtains magical elixirs, one can live to two hundred and forty years, and even up to four hundred and sixty years. In such cases, one would have unimpaired vision and hearing, without senility, and those who fully understand the principles can live forever, but they will not become immortals. The way to preserve life is simply not to harm oneself.

Historical Spirit Seer Historical Spirit Seer, a man from the ancient state of Shu, was named Weichuan. In his seventies, he lived alone in a few rooms in the eastern market, spending his time only on arranging paper money on his bed. No one knew what his methods were, but people called him "Spirit Seer." The people of Shu all revered him. Some said he judged the afterlife and was called a Judge of the Underworld. There was a man surnamed Li who once respected him and accompanied him to the wine shop in front of the house of the Guo family in the southern market. There, the Judge of the Underworld bowed in the air. Li asked him, and he replied: "There are officials from the Water Court here. In three days, there will be a great flood." The Guo family went out of the city to watch the flood, and their horse, startled, fell into the river and drowned them. From this, the people of Shu revered him even more. Later, I saw in the *Daozang Zhenhao* (True Scriptures of the Daoist Canon) that those who practice good deeds in secret, follow the Dao, and revere immortals are granted positions in the underworld in life. This must be the case with Historical Spirit Seer. Historical Spirit Seer was once known to me as the Gate Official of the Celestial Bureaucracy, the Great Xixi. I have also heard that there are living people who judge the afterlife, but they all fear that people will know about it and dare not disclose it carelessly. This is not the case with Historical Spirit Seer, why is that?
