**女先生** A Female Taoist Master In the late period of the Daiping Xingguo era, a female Taoist from Suizhou, whose name is not recorded, passed through Chengdu. She traveled to Qingcheng and various sacred sites. Her demeanor was ancient and elegant, and she enjoyed discussing esoteric Taoist teachings. Though her appearance was youthful, around twenty years old, she did not eat or drink, claiming to have mastered the mysteries of cinnabar. Accompanying her was an old man with white hair and a hunched back, who performed tasks such as burning incense and sweeping. He served behind the female Taoist but was often scolded or abused. Another Taoist, Zhang Dao, a scholar of the Five Classics, was also her attendant. He was nearing forty and was known as "Zhang Wujing." He claimed that the female Taoist was one hundred and twenty years old, and the old servant was actually her distant descendant. The people of Shu City looked up to her and followed her teachings. Many came to her to buy elixirs, seek her as a teacher, or learn fasting techniques, just as they would go to a market. At that time, the governor, Xin Zhongfu, feared she was a witch and ordered her to leave the city, allowing her to travel to various sacred sites. She still had followers accompanying her. After several years, a scholar from Suizhou named Liu arrived in the city and recounted the story of the female Taoist. The scholar laughed and said, "It was only then that she and Zhang Dao were in a romantic relationship. They are still together at the Gengchu site." He revealed that the old servant was actually the father of the female Taoist. Alas, those who follow her should only maintain sincerity and respect, lest they be deceived like children, and thus avoid being misled.

A Deaf Monk Named Li A monk named Ci Yuan from the Three Saints Temple in Guangdu County, the pseudo-Sichuan region, had a little bit of literary knowledge and memorized a lot. His teacher was named Si Zao, a foolish man. Ci Yuan often belittled his teacher, saying: It's a pity that Ci Yuan became a disciple of this monk. He would think about the transformation of the Earth Goddess while walking or sitting. His teacher tried to stop him, but he became even more preoccupied, showing no respect. One day, he loudly called out, and someone in the air slapped his ears, causing him to become deaf for more than twenty years. During the Kai Bao period of the Song Dynasty, he lived in the Yi Jing Temple in Chengdu. A benefactor asked him to recite the Sutra. A monk sitting next to him secretly looked at it and found the volumes were not similar; they were actually the true scriptures of the Southern Hu. He then spoke to the benefactor: People today like to donate gold, silk, and painted images of Buddha, wanting to admire ancient sages and wise men. They have great merit and benefit the people, placing them on the walls and looking at their images to inspire future generations, and say they will gain blessings. How foolish is that? They do not think that ancient sages and wise men all have their words and deeds recorded in books. There are more than five thousand volumes of teachings, clearly recorded, which they cannot read themselves. They cannot understand the principles of cultivation, but they call others to read them, and say they will gain blessings, even more foolish. People of the time called him Monk Qing Jia.

Gou Sheng In the Great Holy Compassion Temple of Yizhou, which was built during the flourishing Kaiyuan period, the surrounding corridors and halls are adorned with famous paintings from successive dynasties, excelling in the southern region. On the eastern corridor, there is a hall dedicated to the bodhisattva Vimalakirti, painted by the Tang dynasty artist Li Hongdu, whose brushwork is unparalleled. On the day of the Ghost Festival, scholars and commoners visit the temple. Three young men, all skilled in music and rhythm, come to this place and point to the music of the celestial musician, saying: "This is the first beat of the second section of the *Nishang Yuyi Qu*." Among them, Gou Sheng says: "I do not care for music, but to marry a wife as beautiful as the celestial musician holding a zither would be enough." He then picks a piece of soil from the painting and swallows it as a joke. Afterward, they all return home. That night, Gou Sheng dreams of being inside the Weimo Hall, where he sees a woman of extraordinary beauty, her radiance dazzling. She leads him to intimate relations under the window. From then on, she suddenly appears to him every night, sometimes in the temple, where they are tender and affectionate.

After a month, the protagonist's maternal uncle, a scholar named Fan, noticed that the protagonist's spirit was troubled and seemed to be under the influence of an evil aura. Some suggested that wearing talismans, taking medicines, and performing religious rituals might dispel the negative influence, and only then would the protagonist recover. The parents agreed, and that night, the celestial maiden wept bitterly, unable to contain her sorrow, and said to the protagonist: "I was originally a servant of the god of the heavens. I admired you so much that I wished to stay by your side, entrusting myself to a spiritual bond. Now that you doubt me, I cannot remain. You do not need to take any talismans or medicines. I do not wish to forget our bond." She then removed a pair of jade fingernail guards and said, "These are to serve as a token of remembrance. You should cherish and protect them. From now on, we part forever." The protagonist held the jade fingernail guards silently, unable to respond, and both of them wept. After she left, the protagonist gradually became weaker and died within a month. The jade fingernail guards were collected by the family, but they were lost during the Red Turban Rebellion. The painting of the celestial maiden, which still exists today, bears nail marks on her neck.

Volume Five Li Haiyang The Daoist Li Haiyang, whose father held a military position during the time of the pseudo-Shu state, followed his father to garrison at the Sword Gate when the heavenly troops attacked Shu. After the Shu army dispersed, father and son returned to the ancient tomb outside the eastern gate of the city of Sichuan at Dingcun Village. They suddenly heard an unusual fragrance emanating from within the tomb. On a clear day, through a small gap, they saw some partially decayed bones, with a cluster of yellow powder beside them. Upon clearing the area, they discovered three small pieces of realgar. Li Haiyang's father was fond of alchemy and knew that the realgar in the tomb could be useful, so he wrapped it in his sleeve. One night, they heard human speech. The father and son asked, "Is the speaker a ghost?" The voice replied, "I am not a ghost; I am a person from the Song dynasty. My family has held official positions, but I did not enjoy such roles. I withdrew to practice Daoism in Chuchou, where I have a villa slightly away from the noise and dust. I can obtain rare metals and minerals, and if any alchemist wishes to conduct experiments, I will provide the necessary materials and furnaces, and help them complete their work."

At that time, Emperor Dezong suspected that Wei Zhongling in Shu was allied with the barbarians, so he ordered me to become a Taoist priest and go to Sichuan to meet Zhongling, to spy on his movements and activities. In the third year of the Huangguan era, he sent another monk named Xingqin to Sichuan to spy on Zhongling. At first, he talked about the hardships of emptiness, later he spoke about alchemy and transmutation. Zhongling tested him repeatedly, and all his claims were verified. For three years, Zhongling treated him with great respect and sincerity. One day, he spoke about the method of the elixir of immortality, and Zhongling became even more respectful and reverent. After the elixir was completed, Zhongling fasted and took it. At first, he felt a sense of clarity and his desires doubled, and the monk then left. In the late spring of the twentieth year of the Zhenyuan era, the poison from the elixir took effect, and he died. I was involved with Xingqin, and thus suffered this fate, and I was trampled by woodcutters and shepherds, and my remains were secretly damaged and decayed. I remained angry and discontented. The father of Haiyang said: "You have been dead for a long time, why don't you return to the world of the living, and why do you stay in the underworld for so long?" He replied: "I once met a high-ranking scholar who taught me the way of refining the body through the shadow. So I found a cave in the deep mountains and valleys of my villa in Chuqiu, and he instructed me to hold onto it for six years, and to be careful not to let anything interfere with it. When the six years are up, I will be welcomed back here with clothes."

The person initially had damaged limbs, but only the internal organs remained unchanged. I then followed the instructions and sealed him, and at the appointed time, upon opening and seeing him, he was completely restored. He sat upright in a small, enclosed room, with hair hanging down and black, a straight and coarse beard, and a lustrous face and appearance, better than when he was first seen. I prepared warm water and new clothes for him and said, "If this is done three times, then transcending the world will be complete." I received this method, and now my form is incomplete. I have transformed from being formless to being formed through practice. Thus, ascending to heaven and descending to earth, any transformation is possible. My form is not yet complete, but I can fly freely, moving from darkness to light, and high-ranking positions do not bring joy to me. I have escaped the toils of the world, and there is no need to lower my aspirations. My current death is not worse than my past life? The elder from Haiyang asked, "Dare I ask what kind of medicine is in the collar of the robe?" He replied, "I often followed Taoists into the mountains to refine elixirs, repaired and maintained the alchemical furnace, burned wood and stoked the bellows, and did not spare any effort."

I often lament the brevity of time and the aging of my body. The most renowned elixir of immortality, the Nine-Refined Elixir, is said to be derived from the divine water of the flower pool; secondly, there is cinnabar and mica, which, when taken, may not grant one the ability to ride clouds and drive phoenixes or command spirits and ghosts, but they can still eliminate all diseases and enhance longevity. I obtained the method of refining cinnabar and took it from the age of twenty until I was forty, gaining its power. If I were to refine it further with fire and ingest it with water, I might hope to catch a glimpse of the Dao. Haiyang's father told me: "I have heard the rules for taking medicine, but I have rarely heard of the way to refine the body." Before he could finish speaking, the dawn came and people began to move about, and I feared being searched, so I did not have time to listen fully, and we parted ways, finding a place to hide. After that, I did not return to that place. Haiyang's father passed away during the Guangde period, and Haiyang followed his teachings, taking and refining cinnabar, wearing the clothes of a Taoist, seeking out a teacher and inquiring about the Dao for over twenty years. He abstained from food, drinking only wine. His clothes and skin always had the scent of cinnabar. During the Chunhua period, he sold elixirs under the Jinjiang Bridge in Yizhou (present-day Chengdu), and his bones and muscles were light and healthy. In the year of the Horse, foreign invaders entered the city, but Haiyang did not leave his room, sitting calmly on a rope bed, and was killed by the bandits. Alas!

White Toad** In the Shu Han state, at the gate of Ji Yanqiu, in front of the Yan Zhen Temple, there was a market where silkworms were sold. A village man was selling a white toad, which was very large and had eyes like red rubies. Those who gathered around it all said it was a fleshly ginseng. There was a medical practitioner named Wang, whose name is unknown, who bought it for one string of cash and returned home. Fearing that it might run away or hide, he placed it in a large stone mortar on the ground. By dusk, the stone mortar became as transparent as a candle lantern. Wang was startled and then performed a ritual purification, chose a day, carried a pot and the toad, and left home to go to Qingcheng Mountain. He was lost without any news. By the next year, when the current dynasty attacked Shu, it was still unknown whether Wang was alive or dead. **Xianyu the Elder** Xueshe Mountain, formerly known as Shihu Mountain, was once the site where Zhang Baizi attained enlightenment and ascended to heaven on the third day of the third month. Today, there is the Zhenzhen Temple on the mountain, which is the remains of that place. Every year on this day, all the people in the city, including officials and commoners from four counties, went to the sacred temple to pray for a good harvest of silkworms. It was spring, and the flowers and trees were in full bloom. The governor and his officials, accompanied by musicians and courtesans, left the city and arrived at the location, where the streets were crowded with carriages, horses, and people. There was an elder named Xianyu Xi, who, along with several friends, drank freely at Wansui Pool. He scooped up some water from the pool and saw a small white toad among the grass on the shore, so he took it. At the table, there was a person surnamed Liu, whose name is unknown, who insisted on seeing it. Xianyu stubbornly refused to give it to him, so the toad bit Xianyu's hand and tried to swallow it. Xianyu teased him, saying: "If you swallow this white toad and become enlightened, you will only become a thief." After swallowing it, he hurriedly drank water, saying: "The toad is in my chest, and I don't know what to do; I feel dizzy and confused." He returned home after more than ten days and was finally treated and recovered.

休复曾览《抱朴子内篇》云，肉芝者谓万歳蟾蜍也。头上有角，目赤，颔下有丹纹，体重而跳捷，以五月五日午时取之，阴干，以左足画地成泉。带之辟兵。若敌人射已，弓弩皆反自伤焉。今人以白虾蟇为肉芝，生吞熟啖者，愚之甚也。设使白虾蟇是肉芝，市井之民但知锥刀之利，嗜欲无厌，藏腑滓秽之气与灵物相攻，水火交战，宁有全人乎。太平兴国末，休复与处士胡本立、进士史载许、僧隐峦往双流县保国观，看古栢树，道逢友人袁德隆，从者于担悬一虾蟇，大如扇许，人皆骇视之。后月余再见袁，因问向者虾蟇所在，袁曰：是荷担者获于田隧中，将归杀而食之。其夜无疾大呌数声而卒。 Hui Fu once read in the *Baopu Zi Neipian* that the meat fungus is said to be the thousand-year toad. It has horns on its head, red eyes, and red markings under its chin; it is heavy yet agile in its jumps. It should be collected at noon on the fifth day of the fifth month, dried in the shade, and its left foot can draw a spring on the ground when placed there. Wearing it protects one from weapons. If an enemy shoots at the wearer, the bow and arrows will backfire and injure the shooter. Nowadays, people consider white toads to be meat fungi, and those who eat them raw or cooked are extremely foolish. If the white toad were indeed a meat fungus, common people would only know the small but sharp profits, with insatiable desires, and the impure qi from their internal organs would clash with the sacred beings, leading to a battle of water and fire. How could anyone survive such a conflict? At the end of the Taiping Xingguo era, Hui Fu, along with the recluse Hu Benli, the scholar Shi Zai Xu, and the monk Yin Luan, went to the Baoguo Temple in Shuangliu County to view ancient cypress trees. On the way, they met a friend named Yuan Delong. A follower carried a toad on his shoulder, about the size of a fan, which startled everyone. A month later, they saw Yuan again and asked about the toad's whereabouts. Yuan replied: "The toad was caught in a field tunnel by the one carrying the load, who intended to kill and eat it. That night, he died suddenly after making loud cries without any apparent illness."

**Eating Toads and Wild Mushrooms** Recently, there was a scholar who enjoyed eating eels, turtles, and toads. He once said: "These three things should not be killed, as the larger ones have poison that can kill people. The smaller ones also cause people to have difficulty urinating, abdominal pain, and even death." It is advisable to pour a large amount of soybeans into half a bowl of freshly drawn water and soak until the soybean water becomes concentrated, then drink it all at once, and the illness will be cured. During the reign of Emperor Taizong of Song, a family named Zhi in the city of Chengdu set up a vegetarian feast at Zhaoguo Temple. They bought wild mushrooms, some of which were black with spots and others were yellowish-white with red patches, and used them as part of the meal. All the monks who ate the mushrooms began to vomit and have diarrhea, and some even died. At that time, a doctor quickly told them: "Just dig a pit in the ground, pour freshly drawn water into it, stir it, and let it settle. This is called 'earth water.' Each person should drink a small cup of it, and no more than three times, and the poison will be neutralized." This method saved many people at the time. Mushrooms that grow on trees are called "rui," and those that grow on the ground are called "jun." Both are products of dampness and moisture. There are also mushrooms that grow on decaying corpses and poisonous snakes. Some are large and bright, and people often mistake them for "lingzhi," believing they can grant immortality, but they actually cause death. Therefore, this is written to warn people not to be misled.

A Rainbow During the month of June in the year of Renchen during the Chuncheng period, a rainbow appeared. At that time, there was a sudden heavy rain. My friend Li Hao Yuan said: The rainbow is the essence of yin and yang. The rainbow is male, and the corona is female. They have colors of green and red, and they often appear during the day with dark clouds, but they do not appear in heavy rain. After sunset, the rainbow appears moving from west to east, and it always appears in pairs. The brighter one is male, and the fainter one is female. When the rainbow enters a house and drinks water, it can bring either good or bad fortune. One person named Chen Jihe said: In the past, when Wei Zhongling was the governor of Shu, he held a banquet in the west pavilion. Suddenly, a storm came, and a rainbow descended into the pavilion, drinking the food on the table. The rainbow had a donkey-like head and a color like a clear sky. Wei Zhongling was afraid and considered it an omen of disaster, so he stopped the banquet. One of the guests said: Why are you worried? It is a true omen of good fortune. The rainbow is a messenger from heaven. When it appears in the wrong place, it brings misfortune, but when it appears in the right place, it brings good fortune. The principle is clear.

He is a just man, and this is indeed a good omen. I dare to congratulate him in advance. After ten days, he was appointed as the Minister of the Central Secretariat. During the reign of Meng, Xu Guangpu's residence saw a rainbow enter a well and drink water. His mother said: "During the Shu Kingdom, a rainbow entered our well, and the first emperor of Shu took a daughter from our family as a concubine. Now it has entered our home again, so there will certainly be a daughter who becomes a concubine and a son who becomes a general or chancellor, this is a sign of good fortune." Not even a full ten days later, her daughter was selected to enter the palace, and later followed the Shu emperor back to the capital, becoming the Huifei. His mother often said: "I am from the Cheng family of Guizhi Village, Meishan County, Mianzhou. My uncle served in the Han Baozhen's military camp during the false Shu period, serving as the county magistrate of Meishan in Mianzhou. When he returned to the countryside to mourn his mother's death, his mourning period was about to end. It was the end of summer, and sometimes dark clouds appeared over the house, all were like the late afternoon sun, shining with red and green hues. The sky cleared up after a brief rain, and the water in the jars, pots, and well dried up. It rained heavily. Not long after, Han Shizhong was appointed as the governor of Qin Prefecture, and my uncle was appointed as the deputy governor of the military governor. It is known that rainbows are often omens."

**Avoiding Lightning** In the fifth month of the summer in the year "丙申" during the "至道" era, the entertainer Luomeichang had a relative living in the village of Nanguo Jingkou Zang. Early in the morning, Luomeichang went to visit his relative. At that time, some villagers had caught several fish. Luomeichang took three of them and threaded them through the umbrella. On his way back, the sky became dark and gloomy, and a sudden and intense thunderstorm broke out. Trees all fell, and the ground was illuminated by firelight. Luomeichang feared that the fish might be a dragon and threw them into the fields. The thunder and lightning became more intense, and he was frightened and ran to a village house to hide, shaking uncontrollably and unable to stop. He waited until the storm cleared before returning. The next day, when it was still early morning, the villagers brought back the umbrella and the fish, saying, "Last night, the master of the village sent me to find you, fearing that you might have been frightened by the storm. The thunderbolt struck the umbrella shaft and took the mischievous dragon away. The fish and the umbrella were burned by the thunderfire. I picked them up and am now bringing them back."

In the summer of the sixth month of the year Wu Zi during the reign of Emperor Duangong, a violent storm with thunder and lightning struck the door of the Hall of Arhats at Shengxing Temple, causing the house pillar to break. Three monks fell to the ground, their bodies as if burned. According to tradition, the mischievous dragon, troubled by its duty to bring rain, hides in many places, sometimes inside a person's body, or within ancient wooden pillars and the eaves of towers and pavilions, and must be captured by the thunder god. If it finds itself in the open fields with no place to escape, it will enter the horn of a cow or the body of a herdsboy, often being troubled by this creature and being struck dead by lightning. Clowns escaped by fleeing, but these monks did not flee and were struck dead. The saying goes: "When there is a sudden thunderstorm and strong wind, one must change." The *Book of Changes* says: "Repeated thunder warns the gentleman to be fearful and self-examine," meaning that a gentleman is always trembling and cautious, not daring to be lazy, seeing the anger of heaven and fearing the power of thunder, fearing that punishment will reach oneself. The poem says: "Revering the anger of heaven and not daring to be frivolous, revering the changes of heaven and not daring to rush." Is this what it means?

Hailstorm In the year 1009 AD during the reign of Emperor Zhenzong of Song, a man named Pang Yongxian was living temporarily in Guangdu County. On the fourth month of summer as the day was drawing to a close, suddenly violent winds and thunderbolts tore off roofs and uprooted trees. A hailstorm followed, lasting until dawn. The next morning, he inquired of travelers, who said that the hail had spread across dozens of miles from the eastern mountain of the county, moving southwest along the river, and its extent was unknown. Wherever the hail fell, fences, walls, houses, and large trees were all struck by hail and uprooted by lightning. Cattle, horses, dogs, and pigs were all startled and fell to the ground; birds and small animals that were hit all died. At that time, the wheat was just ripening, and there was none left. One villager said: "That night, many hailstones fell through my house, as large as beans, shattering bowls, jars, pots, and cauldrons." Where the hail fell, only about two or three out of ten trees, houses, and tiles remained. Hail is frozen rain, formed by the interaction of yin and yang. The *Zuo Zhuan* states: "All hail is winter's excess of yang and summer's hidden yin. When a sage is in power, there is no hail, and even if there is, it does not cause disaster." This is because, when the common people enjoy a bountiful harvest, they then engage in luxury, leading to clothing and ornaments, carriages and horses, and houses and buildings violating regulations, scattering and discarding the five grains without any care or respect. Heaven issues a warning to punish them.

De Jian, whose name is unknown, was given the nickname "Jian" because of his loud voice. Originally from a prominent family in Chengdu, he did not engage in regular occupations but had a strong interest in falconry, keeping hawks and encouraging those who could follow beasts with eagles and dogs to be his companions. During the Yongxi period, he went hunting with his hawks and dogs at Xueshe Mountain. An eagle caught a male pheasant, and he saved it, allowing it to survive. The pheasant had two spurs on each foot, which his companions found strange. He wrapped it in a cloth and carried it. He noticed it gradually warming up. Within one li, it was like fire. While wandering, suddenly it became dark and gloomy, and a severe storm of wind, thunder, and hail ensued, causing the trees to sway violently. He did not know where to go and abandoned the pheasant at the bottom of a ravine, running to the True Temple to take shelter. The rain was pouring down, and it cleared up in the middle of the night. He could not bear the shock. At that time, there was a recluse named Fan who heard his story and said, "The pheasant is a dragon. Dragons are the leaders among the five types of creatures, without a fixed form, residing in twelve positions, and transforming into chickens, pigs, cattle, and horses. This dragon has become a pheasant, it is a transformation. It brought upon itself its own troubles. If there were no changes in the weather, it would also be hard to escape the cooking pot and cutting board."

A Tale of the Old Man Li The surname of the person is not recorded, and people all call him the wild man of Yuan, who once lived in a village in Guangdu County. When it was very hot, there was an old man dressed in white who came to visit Yuan's village. Yuan invited him to sit down and said to him, "I am from the Li family, and my home is to the south of this county. I come with a special request to a gentleman. I hope you will have mercy and forgive me. There will be a generous reward." Yuan did not agree much to it, but he just comforted him. He then stayed to eat some rice porridge with salted soybeans and left. After three more days, due to heavy rain, the stream overflowed. The villagers caught a carp using their nets, about three feet long, with scales and fins like gold, and it kept flapping and struggling. Yuan called his servant to cut it up, and inside the fish, there was a little rice and salted soybeans. Yuan then realized that the old man was a fish, and he said, "Old Man Li, though clever, could not escape my one drink." Some say, "No creature is wiser than the dragon." That fish is a divine dragon. Such transformations, how could they be difficult to escape? Is it that wisdom has its limits and divinity has its shortcomings? Alas, in times of hardship and distress, ordinary people and saints, dragons, and small creatures are all equal. How can they be different in being spared or not spared?

The Caring Mother Pool The Caring Mother Pool, also known as the Lush Pool, is located about seventy to eighty li into the mountains from Yongkang City. The pool water is clear and bright, with its depth unknown. Every autumn, the wind causes leaves to fall, but there has never been any grass or trees floating on its surface. If even a small leaf or tiny particle falls, it is always carried away by flying birds. On clear and bright days, the water surface shows five colors like spread brocade. If wood or stones are thrown into it, black smoke immediately rises, followed by thunder, lightning, rain, and hail. In years of drought, prayers and sacrifices are always answered. I have seen someone from the Daoist community visiting a dragon, and water with five colors and sand on the stones indicates the presence of a dragon. These are places where dragons live.

The Dragon Maiden Hall In the northwest corner of Yizhou City, there stands a shrine dedicated to the Dragon Maiden. This shrine was established in the 28th year of the Kaiyuan era by Zhang Qiu Gong Jianqiong, the Governor of the region, who had successfully captured the city of Pingrong. He dreamed of a maiden who said, "I am the dragon of this city. I now abandon the remote corners of the frontier to embrace the culture of the Tang Dynasty." He consulted various shamans, and their responses were consistent. Soon after, a memorial was submitted to establish the shrine, which was bestowed the title of Huichang Shrine. The shrine is located near the former residence of Yang Xiong in the Lesser City. Prayers are offered at the shrine during droughts and floods, and they are always answered. During the Ganfu period, Duke Gao of Yan, Gao Pian, constructed the outer city, which included the Dragon Maiden Shrine within its walls. As the construction workers set up scaffolding, sudden storms occurred, causing the work to be completed in the morning and destroyed by evening. This was reported to Duke Gao, who also dreamed of the Dragon Maiden, who said, "I am the dragon from the Dragon Mother Pool in the Western Mountains. You are now building a city. Please move my shrine outside the gate, so that it may be convenient for travel." Duke Gao agreed in his dream. Upon waking, he ordered the shrine to be moved outside the city walls and had it rebuilt. The storms ceased, and the city was no longer destroyed. Subsequently, Kings Wang and Meng greatly decorated the shrine. Prayers offered at the shrine were answered, and the Dragon Maiden was bestowed the title of Wise and Holy Lady. In the year Jiwu of Tianxi, there was no rain from September until February of the following year. Prayers were offered at temples and shrines, but there was no effect. Censor-in-Chief Zhao Zhen of the Prefecture personally visited the shrine and offered secret prayers. Before reaching the prefecture, a heavy rain fell all night, and all the counties reported sufficient rain. That year was a bountiful harvest, and the people were free from disease. Thus, a memorial was submitted to renovate the shrine.

Chapter Six Elegy for Shu The *Zuo Zhuan* says: "Natural disasters occur in every country over time." During the Jia Wu year of the *Chunhua* era in Yizhou, bandits arose in Qing and Shu, surrounding and besieging the city walls. The commander-in-chief, lacking defensive preparations, fled to Jianmen Pass. The rebels took advantage of the situation to enter the city, and burning, looting, killing, and injuring were extremely severe. In the south, dozens of military states were all under the control of the rebels, while only Mei, Ling, Zizhou, and Suizhou defended themselves with strong walls. The rebels occupied Yizhou for a hundred days. When the imperial troops arrived, they killed all the rebels without leaving any survivors. Wherever the army passed, everything was left as thorns and brambles. The court appointed Zhang Yong, a scholar-official and Langzhong of the Yumu Department, as the governor of Yizhou. Upon his arrival, he observed the people's suffering and deeply understood the causes of the rebellion, composing an elegy for Shu in forty rhyming lines. Now it is recorded in full. The preface says:

In the first month of spring in the first year of the *Zhidao* era, when the Office of Review and Evaluation was assessing officials, I was summoned to address the Emperor, saying: Heaven has turned away from the Western Shu, and the year is marked by repeated famines. The appointment of officials has failed; corrupt governance and embezzlement abound. The people have risen in complaint and lamentation, giving rise to rebellion and violence. I have been commanded to lead the elite military force to destroy the wicked and rebellious. How much more so for the common people, who cannot sustain their lives. Observing how people are treated and how the people are treated, my intention is not to be lazy. Leniency breeds corruption, severity harms the people. He who can balance these extremes is the one who truly embodies this. You are upright and just, with a record of success in governance. Go to the distant and remote regions of Qiong and Bo and govern their customs. Be reverent and loving, you must be diligent. Reverently accept your mission, and proceed westward in your chariot. On the twenty-eighth day of the fourth month of summer, you shall assume your duties. Alas, my plans are ordinary and shallow, yet I dare not be negligent. The powerful and deceitful are restrained, and thus taxation is reduced. Provide for the poor, comfort the displaced, eliminate exploitation, and promote the Emperor’s virtue. After a year, the people still cannot find peace; this is not the fault of the county officials. Who among those who listen does not know the people’s hearts? The people fear the Emperor’s soldiers’ plundering from above and the bandits’ violence from below! The good families, now impoverished, gradually return to banditry; they would rather die than suffer—this is deeply lamentable. The Emperor is far away in the nine heavens, and the poor and lowly fear the powerful and dare not speak. Alas, though the office of collecting poems has long been abandoned, the path of poetry and satire must not be silent. I dare to compose a forty-verse elegy for Sichuan, inscribe it on the hall of governance, and I beg the wise men not to consider me a fool or a blind man for my words:

Shu State was wealthy and populous, with customs that were proud and superficial. Luxury reached its peak with pearls and shells, and excessive indulgence in entertainment prevailed. A rainbow bridge spewed flying water, while smoke and willows closed the vermilion pavilions. The shadows of candles chased the sinking stars, and songs harmonized with the setting moon. Fighting cocks broke a million coins, and shouting and cursing filled the air at the game. Young women adorned with white jade earrings and charmingly adorned horses with golden harnesses roamed. Taverns remained open at night, and spring flowers made one feel ashamed. Rice fields stretched to the evening clouds, and silk and embroidery interwove with gentle breezes. Prosperous for thirty years, scenes seemed like yesterday. Heaven's way inherently harms excess, and excess inevitably leads to disaster. Those who governed at that time did not think to alleviate the people's suffering, could not promote pure governance, and could not change customs back to frugality. Character was not straightforward, often indulged in music and beauty, followed desires and gained empty praise, and followed the crowd and indulged in greed. Devoured the flesh of living beings like silkworms, exercised power and committed violence, and flattery deceived the emperor's ears, seeking profit only through exploitation. Fury arose in one place, and thousands of miles away, people raised their arms and leaped. Fire heated the cold sky, and snowy light flickered over lotus swords. No one could repel the enemy, and no time to beat the drum.

Those who harm others and are greedy for wealth are all met with sharp blades shining; broken tiles and bricks accumulate on terraces and pavilions, thorns obscure the city walls, the neighborhood is locked in desolation, and the courtyard is noisy with sparrows. A measure of grain is exchanged for gold and silk, while straw is gambled for fine silk. Alas, the proud and extravagant people cannot be satisfied with humble vegetables; the court orders the supreme general to lead the army to purge the root evil. The fierce troops arrive, and the nest of the fierce bird is how weak; the flames of burning hair flicker, and the sharp edge of the bamboo breaks and glows. The soldiers are proud and cannot be restrained, killing people as if it were a game; the young and old all suffer punishment, and how to distinguish between jade and stone? Unable to punish the strong and violent, they rush to seize and plunder, leaving the livelihoods of the good people empty, and the heart of the debtor dies. The four fields are filled with wolves, and who will cultivate the five acres? The common people are not at peace, burning as if sitting in a cauldron. The army does not follow the law, and how to repel the remaining evils? To make the fierce bees and scorpions thrive, with few calculations, they can be controlled. The border is not yet pacified, and how can one eat the heavenly reward? The world is now obsessed with competition, and who will restore the reputation of the honest and upright? The yellow canopy is far away ten thousand miles, and the nine layers are high and vast. The times are said to have many heroes, and talent is not lacking in Wei and Huo. Recently heard the order to appoint good ministers, look forward to seeing the extraordinary strategies.

Ai Yanzuo, a lacquer craftsman from Chengdu, was driven by bandits to the prefectural office in the year of the horse and ordered to make lacquerware. On the sixth day of May, someone heard the sound of drums and war drums, and the southern gate caught fire, indicating that the heavenly soldiers had arrived at the prefectural office. Ai Yanzuo climbed a tree and hid among the dense leaves, where he saw the heavenly soldiers moving around, searching, capturing, and killing. At night, he climbed down from the tree and lay among the corpses. At midnight, he heard calls that were similar to those of generals and officials. There were several people, and they had no candles or torches. He secretly observed them, but could not see their forms, only hearing them read names from records. The corpses heard the calls and responded one by one. Only without calling out the name of Ai Yanzuo and passing over him. The corpses were piled up, but the inspection continued. He then understood that the righteous court was punishing rebels and traitors according to heaven's will, and thus there was no mistake.

**The Barbarian Woman** In the fifth month of the year Jia Wu, the heavenly troops conquered Yiquan. By August, the rebel faction Zhi Jin still held Jiazhou, and General Shu Chongyi Han led troops to attack them. When the army reached Hongya, a soldier captured a woman of a non-Han ethnicity, who was quite beautiful. He placed her beneath his tent and repeatedly tried to force himself upon her, claiming she was already married. She responded by crossing her arms, pressing her knees together, and sitting on the ground. The soldier became angry and allowed her to be beheaded and have her heart cut out, yet she could not be broken. She steadfastly resisted the violence, and her resistance grew stronger. For three days she neither ate nor drank, and then she followed this with death, yet still, the violation of propriety did not occur. The commander, upon hearing of this, felt pity and ordered that she be returned to her family. Alas, though a woman of the so-called barbarians, she was able to remain steadfast, and the violent could not defile her. If the people of the Central Kingdom have no people of integrity, are they not ashamed? **Zhang Guangzan** Zhang Guangzan was a descendant of Zhang Luohan of Jinshui Shicheng Mountain, and because he was skilled in painting Arhats, he was named after this talent. He often painted in temples and monasteries, spending many springs and autumns in this work, eating with the monks. His nature was cautious, sincere, and he adhered to the Way without excess. In this way, he lived for more than fifty years, and all people respected him. In the year Jia Wu, he was captured by the rebels and forced to present his neck. After several sword strikes, his neck did not break, and so he lay among the corpses. At midnight, he saw an old monk who said, "Because you have devoted your life to painting merit, I have come to save you." After the monk spoke, he opened his eyes and felt no pain. To this day, the scar on his neck remains. Indeed, the clear mercy of the Western saints is evident—could this not be a sign?

Golden Wheel According to the *Beimeng Suoyan*, during the reign of Emperor Xiantong, a high-ranking military official named Gao governed the western part of Sichuan. In Ya Zhou, there was a Daoist practitioner named Wang Jian, whose reputation reached the Bohai Kingdom. The king of Bohai sent a messenger through a person named Lü Shang to summon Wang Jian. When Lü arrived, Wang Jian and his wife lived in a humble thatched cottage with a single bed, separated by a screen. The wife asked, "What will we use to entertain the guest?" Wang Jian replied, "There is a banquet today at the residence of the city's high-ranking official. You can go and fetch the supplies." Soon, the wine and food arrived, arranged in a splendid manner, not something that could be hastily prepared. Considering the distance, it was more than a hundred li away, Lü was astonished. Wang Jian smiled and said, "The king of the southern barbarians once cast a golden wheel and asked me to exchange it for the golden wheel on the tower of the Fugan Temple in Chengdu. The people of Shu did not know about this, and at that time, about sixteen out of seventeen people revered it. By the fifth year of the Chonghua era, bandits entered the city, and the temple was destroyed by fire. The golden wheel fell to the ground intact, and it was made of bronze and iron. It was not replaced by the king of the barbarians with gold. This is a parable of Wang Jian, and the legend about the golden wheel is a significant but uncertain tradition." The golden treasure turned to smoke.

Wang Sheng, a brutish and violent man from Jiangyuan County in Shuzhou, was an accomplice of the bandits Wang Xiaobo and Li Shun. In the year 994 CE, he seized control of Yizhou and was hastily appointed as an instrumentally low-ranking official. He led a group of more than a hundred young men to raid and plunder women and goods, killing without restraint. He forced the poor people in the city to guide him to wealthy households, where they hid in underground cellars. While digging in one of these cellars, he unearthed an ancient hidden treasure of silver, including gold bars and black iron bars, as well as jewelry and other precious items, all in ancient styles. Soon after, he killed those who had guided him, carried more than thirty pieces of gold and silk, and buried them in a mountain cave in Jiangyuan. Those who buried the treasure were also killed later to prevent the secret from being revealed. In the city, Hui's wife, a woman who had been captured by the bandits, was held in their household. Her husband, having no news of her whereabouts, often hired people to search for her in the bandit-controlled areas of Qiong and Shu, but found no trace. It was not until March that he learned she was in the bandit's household. Her husband and his brother, Xie Yuanying, paid with gold and silk to redeem her, but both of them were drowned in the river. In August, the imperial army recaptured Sichuan, and this bandit surrendered, wearing a brocade robe and silver belt. Those who saw him upon entering the city all gnashed their teeth in hatred. Previously, those who surrendered were usually sent to the imperial court, but the bandit discarded his robe and belt and fled back to Jiangyuan. His wife and children reported that the place where the treasure was buried had fire and smoke like a kiln for several days. They secretly went to dig it up, but all of it was gone. In a state of shock, the imperial army captured him and brought him into the city, where he was then punished according to the law. Alas, killing to obtain wealth, the suffering and injustice increased greatly, and it was not for his own use, but he himself was slaughtered. Where the fire and smoke had appeared earlier, the treasures were already gone. I have often heard that treasures hidden in the earth, when occasionally seen, may change their form. Could it not be that they have transformed, or could it be that the spirits have hidden them?

Luxury Does Not Last Long In the year Jia Wu, the rebels attacked the region of Yizhou. There were those who, dissatisfied with their circumstances, joined the rebels, wielding weapons to raid and loot civilian homes. They also joined the rebels in harming the people, demanding more and more without satisfaction. The heavenly troops pacified the rebels and issued a lenient edict, forgiving and not punishing those coerced into following the rebels, releasing them to return to farming. These people merely avoided punishment and, having much wealth, then indulged their hearts and desires, believing they would never be restrained again. They rode in luxury and wore fine clothes, singing, drinking, and entertaining themselves, playing with and enjoying rare and valuable things. They exceeded and were extravagant in funerals, weddings, and engagements, treating relatives as enemies. In no more than a few years, disasters, plagues, and epidemics struck, and public and private disputes arose one after another. All their wealth was exhausted, and their carriages, horses, houses, and buildings all became the property of others. They became poor again as they were at the beginning. Alas, unjustly acquired wealth seems to be under the control of divine forces. Those who do not suffer the consequences and survive are few. When good people become wealthy, it is called heavenly reward; when wicked people become wealthy, it is called heavenly calamity. These people are punished by heaven. Is this what it means?

Liu Xu In the autumn of the eighth month of the Ding You year during the Zhi Dao era, the Inspector of Workshops and Crafts across various states, Han Jingyou, arrived at Huai'an Military Unit. His subordinates, the Guangwu soldiers Liu Xu and others, plotted against and killed him. Han leaped over the wall and escaped. That night, the military rebels plundered Huai'an Military Unit, and by dawn, they captured Jintang Ancient City and Hanshou. In six days, they traveled more than five hundred miles, raiding and looting five military states and ten townships. Wherever they went, the local authorities could not resist, and they forced and looted the military and civilians, with their momentum unstoppable. The prefectures and counties were shocked and intimidated, and the population fled. At that time, the Prefect Zhang Mǐxué told the Pacification Commissioner Shangguan Zheng: "The rebels are in Qingzhou today; tomorrow, they will surely head for Jiamizhou. If they find a place to settle, it will be like a fish in water, and it will be difficult to eliminate them. You will surely regret it. Today, please go immediately and move the troops across the river, attack them head-on, seize their courage, and we will capture all of them. This is the best strategy. Time cannot be missed." Shangguan Zheng then assembled his troops and weapons and went forward. Passing the Xinjin River, he encountered the rebels eating at Fangjing Well and rushed to inform Zhang Mǐxué. Zhang said, "Liu has already entered the well; how could he escape further?" By midday, he reported the victory, killed all of their followers, and returned triumphantly. Zhang Mǐxué foresaw the enemy's movements in advance, as in all such cases. Shangguan Zheng was able to lead his troops, and this action was as easy as breaking dry twigs. The region therefore became orderly.

Volume Seven Lament for a Deceased Friend On the first day of the first month in the Gengzi year of the Xianping era, a detachment of the Divine Guard killed their commander and seized control of Yizhou. In April, the imperial army came to suppress the rebellion, reaching the city walls. The rebels resisted the imperial forces, forcing the elderly and children to climb the walls. The imperial army shouted and surrounded the city, attacking day and night, causing heavy casualties within the city. The rebels demanded supplies, weapons, money, silk, and gold from the civilians, leaving the people unable to sustain their lives. On the 20th day of the ninth month, the imperial army entered the city, and the rebels fled in the night. The commander, remembering the hardships endured by the people in the city, feared that both the good and the bad would be destroyed, and so ordered them to be lured out of the city to be comforted and reassured. Initially, the people in the city were forced by the rebels to carry their elderly and children out of the city, with about two-thirds fleeing to villages and rural areas to escape. Some who fled the city were captured by the rebels and brought back to be killed, while others were forced to work on the city walls and suffered under the rebels' harsh laws. Some were exhausted from giving supplies and suffered brutal treatment, and others who refused to comply were killed or beaten. Some died of illness due to extreme grief and anger, while others collaborated with the rebels, offering advice and accepting bribes. Still others avoided the rebels all day until the city fell without ever seeing the rebels' forces.

In such cases, is fate not from heaven? Is heaven not the source of fate? The candidate Zhang Ji has written a memorial for his deceased friend Yang Xi, and the candidate Peng Cheng has written a biography of his friend Hao Feng. Here, I will record their deeds and include their memorials and biographies. It is not only Yang Xi and Hao Feng. May future people who read them not lament and reflect on acts of rebellion against the sovereign and cruelty to the people? The preface to the memorial for a deceased friend states: My deceased friend Yang Xi, with the courtesy name Xiao Long, was a truly devoted scholar. In the past, he formed literary friendships with Li Tian from Zhaozhou, Ren Jie from Shuzhou, Zhang Kui from Nanyang, and myself, and we all studied Confucian classics in Le'an. The teacher (Yang Xi) devoted himself entirely to the Six Teachings, then studied historical texts, and explored the teachings of all schools of thought, seeking profound and subtle ideas, selecting those that were aligned with the Way. After accumulating knowledge internally, he expressed it externally, and then wrote on paper. He wrote essays examining the actions and words of virtuous scholars and chaste men, clarified the rise and fall of ancient customs, recorded the great and small deeds of righteousness, distinguished between correct and incorrect applications, did not exaggerate praise nor hide faults, so that he could express the aspirations of his heart. Daily adhering to this principle to enter public service, so that he could hold his books without shame, maintaining his words and deeds in harmony with others.

For fifteen years, I have never forgotten this for a single day. My deceased friend among us was particularly quiet and retiring; he did not merely seek advancement to gain fame, fleeting praise, or quick success, but truly awaited the opportunity for local scholars and capable people to present their writings to the Spring Official. Unfortunately, last year, bandits seized the city, and my friend fell ill with worry and could not escape far away. When he eventually took refuge in the Western Mountains, he did not get to say goodbye to my deceased friend. Whenever I think of it, though our paths are separate, our hearts are the same; though our rooms are far apart, we are close in spirit. I hope that we will eventually reunite and fulfill our previous aspirations. When the imperial army defeated the rebels, I returned to the right path, but my friend died of extreme anger. His young son, who was just a child, also died young. Alas, my deceased friend's achievements were already established, but his aspirations were not fulfilled, and his fate was so difficult. He has passed away, and his heir has also died; his place remains remote, and few people know him. What is the purpose of heaven, making those who assist the good and hate the evil suffer so greatly? I wish to record his virtuous deeds and his remaining writings, along with those of my three friends, and present them to the scholars and heroes of the literati, so they will surely recognize and honor him, somewhat alleviating my eternal regret. Now, I will write these words to express my sorrow for our friendship.

I have gained friends through my actions, and joined the community, adhering to righteousness. I have not been ruined by poverty and lowly status, nor am I ashamed to seek fame and profit. I shine like the distant vision of ancient historians, and have savored the true essence of the Six Classics. I have discussed the nine schools of thought in the *Bianji*, and expanded on Liu Xiang's *Qi Zhi*. Having accumulated knowledge within, I now express it outwardly, and am fortunate to enter office and handle affairs. I am about to tie the sash and clean the cap, not for you to soar and fall. Why do bandits and rebels suddenly rise, occupying the fortresses of the frontier regions? Are you merely far away without purpose, or is it that illness makes it hard for you to rise? I go to the West Mountain without bidding you farewell, whether in office or out, our hearts are the same. The turmoil continues, and truly, our meetings are close and intimate. When the imperial army has pacified the rebels, I hear that my friend has passed away. The sparrows chirp, while the crane remains silent; the sick wolves gnaw, and the righteous officer dies in anger. Alas, the lineage of the Qian family is not continued, and the heir is also hindered. Only to cry out to heaven about his death, thus, he was brilliant and swift in spirit. His aspirations are shown in his writings and deeds, to demonstrate to the learned in the marketplace. To know you as such, to ensure virtue is not ashamed. Now, I suppress my sorrow to write these words, to report on my deceased friend's life and death.

**Hao Feng's Biography** Hao Feng, courtesy name Zhi Yao, was from Chengdu. As a youth, he was fond of learning and devoted himself to the study of poetry. His nature was gentle yet lazy, and some said he was timid and incapable of accomplishing anything. He often wished to obtain a local recommendation, but he did not succeed. When bandits rose up in the region, his assets were nearly entirely lost. Despite being tormented by cold and hunger, he showed no signs of worry or complaint. During the Xianping era, a military commander in Shu violated military discipline, leading to chaos as the soldiers turned into bandits, killing the local governor and seizing the city. From spring to autumn, they drove the elderly and children to defend the city. Some offered strategies to the bandits, ordering them to search for all the scholars in the region and appoint them to positions in order to demonstrate their effectiveness. They gathered dozens of such individuals and lined them up with soldiers, threatening them: "Those who do not comply will be executed here, and their families will suffer as well." All were terrified and complied. Hao Feng stepped forward and deceived the bandit leader, saying: "Considering the scholars you are seeking, I am not a scholar, how could I seek your salary?" He refused to obey the order. His words and tone were resolute and filled with anger, unyielding and unbreakable. The bandit leader, enraged, ordered him to be taken away. Three times, as he was about to be executed, he was called back, but each time, his words remained the same as before. By chance, he was released by the bandits, though he was beaten with a stick. After being spared, he hid in his home. When the imperial troops arrived, the rebel forces were completely destroyed. Some reported this to the local governor, who submitted the matter to the authorities, and the affair was halted. Hao Feng also did not speak of it again. He lived in poverty as he always had, remaining composed and undisturbed.

Ah, at that time, those in power still lived by chance, while I, a mere scholar, could risk my life to eliminate the wicked and avoid falling into injustice. They flaunted their wealth and power for personal gain to please the times, how could they not feel ashamed! I lived a humble life, concealing my identity and blending in with the crowd, and was not well-known. Ah, how difficult it is to know whether a person's reputation is based on sincerity. In my hometown, everyone thought I was cowardly, but it was only when chaos struck that my true character became clear. At that time, there was no other concern but the choice to leave or stay. Leaving was the right thing to do, staying was the wrong thing to do. Leaving the difficult and staying with the easy, being able to do what is hard, holding firm to one's principles, this is also close to wisdom and courage. Loyalty, bravery, and righteousness exist at all times, but whether they are known depends on whether one is fortunate or unfortunate. If Yu Shifu had not met Yanzi, he would have been just a captive; if Nie Zheng had not met his sister, he would have been just an unknown brute. When one is fortunate, it brings great honor; when one is unfortunate, it is trivial. One should not start doing good deeds only when they are fortunate, nor refrain from doing good deeds only because they are unfortunate. A noble person living in obscurity does not lose its fragrance just because there is no one to appreciate it, and a gentleman does not change his principles because of poverty or adversity. If one has good qualities, even if others do not know about it, it is still good. How could it be limited to just receiving blessings? If one has bad qualities, even if others do not know about it, it is still bad. How could it be limited to just suffering misfortune? The *I Ching* says: "A well that is cleaned but not drunk from causes me sorrow; the wise ruler will share in the blessings." It also says: "The ox is bound and its ears are cut off, which is ominous." Is this what is being referred to? As for my conduct, even if it is not widely known, I will certainly consider it known. Therefore, the reputation is based on the reality, and it can also serve as an encouragement.

Chen Jihe Chen Xizai, a Jinshi from the pseudo-Shu regime, was not only well-versed in literature but also the most outstanding in calligraphy and painting. All of these he had seen and recognized. In the prefecture, families with a passion for collecting treasured many items, and Chen Xizai had seen and recognized many of them, making it impossible for authenticity and forgery to escape his detection. After being appointed by a rebel regime and sent to Lianzhou for more than a year, some villagers from the west came to visit. He wrote a letter saying: "When I was at home, I buried an iron arrow bottle at a certain place, filled with copper coins. If this letter reaches home, you can have it dug up." After a while, the letter arrived, and Chen Xizai dug up the bottle at the place mentioned. Inside, he only saw one turtle, just fitting inside the bottle. The next day, when he took a look, the turtle was no longer there, leaving only an empty bottle. The transformation of things, especially common in historical records, cannot be understood by wisdom alone.

In the year Gengzi, bandits in the Yizhou region occupied the city. The main army attacked from outside the northern gate, digging trenches near the city walls and launching an assault. Arrows and stones rained down like a storm. In Zhongba Street, there was an old woman named Wang, over seventy years old, with a grandson or granddaughter aged 14 or 15, who was forced by the bandits to guard the city. The old woman brought food and drink to the guards every day. One day, the bandits gathered musicians and dancers at the entrance of the Wawu Temple. The old woman leaned against a tree and watched. A bandit approached her, turned his back, and sat in a disrespectful manner. The old woman scolded him, but he did not leave, and she cursed him harshly, but he seemed not to hear. The old woman angrily moved away. Shortly after, a cannonball flew through the air and struck the bandit, crushing his head. If not for this bandit, the old woman would have been hit by the projectile. When the city fell, only the old woman remained alive. Now over ninety years old, she is both old and sick, suffering from extreme cold and hunger, and makes straw shoes to support herself. She often tells people that if she had been killed by cannonballs during the siege, she would not be experiencing poverty now. How unfortunate is this? Life and death are determined by fate, as Zixia said that people do not surpass their fate. All people, whether noble or common, rich or poor, meet with good or bad fortune, which is a matter of luck and misfortune. Yan Hui died young, and Confucius said he was unfortunate, a short-lived person. To be unfortunate, then to know that a long life is a blessing. The old woman who sells straw shoes is poor but lives a long life, lamented as unfortunate, indeed.

The Blind Girl In the year of Gengzi, the heavenly troops attacked the region of Yizhou, and the rebels broke through the encirclement and fled at night. The commander showed compassion for the civilians in the city, sending them out to be recruited and persuaded to leave, while the main army was entering to search and capture. After the situation was stabilized, all were allowed to return home. In a ditch near the southern market, there was a blind girl, about seven or eight years old, who called out: "Father? Mother? Brother? Sister-in-law? Where have you gone? You do not provide me with food and drink!" The blind girl, driven by hunger and thirst, did not know she had no family, but she only complained and called out to her parents, brother, and sister-in-law, day and night without ceasing. A neighbor woman said: "This is Sun's daughter, who, at three years old, suffered from flour and beans entering her eyes, causing blindness. Her parents loved her for her intelligence and often taught her to recite Buddhist scriptures, raising her very well. Her father died due to military defeat, her mother died of grief and anger, her sister-in-law died while providing for the laborers, hit by an arrow, her brother was lost when the city fell, and she has no other relatives." The onlookers were deeply moved and shed tears. After ten days, sometimes a neighbor woman would meet the blind girl and ask about her whereabouts.

The neighbor woman said: The blind girl did not accept food or drink from others, but only mourned and called out to her parents, refusing to drink water, and she revived and then died again. She died after seven days. Out of pity, she collected the remaining embers and burned them. She found a one-tael gold piece in the blind girl's clothing and sold it to provide for the painting of a monk. Alas, on the day the city fell, there were many like this! The reason I wrote about the blind girl is that my humble opinion is driven by emotion. If a family is wealthy and has abundant resources, then ritual and righteousness flourish; if wealth is insufficient, then ritual and righteousness both decline. This is the common human sentiment. At that time, the household's property was exhausted, and they were especially distressed. How could it be said that the neighbor woman alone could give up the remaining embers, burn the blind girl, and then, without using the gold for herself, use it to provide for the blind girl and the painting of a monk? What a remarkable neighbor woman! She could maintain such sincerity in times of extreme poverty and distress, so I have written about her particularly. And those who see profit and forget righteousness today, are they not the sinners of this neighbor woman?

Iron-Boned Fish Yu Xuan, whose courtesy name was Xuanzhi, was a native of Chengdu. In the year of Gengzi, he encountered bandits who occupied the city. He told me: "In my family, everything can be disregarded, but the only treasure is one sword." He opened the room to show me. In the darkness, I saw a light several feet long. Upon closer inspection, it was a sword. Then he asked where it was obtained. He said: "My late father passed the imperial examination under the supervision of Jia, the Secretary of the False Shu regime. That winter, he traveled to Qingcheng Mountain, returned to Wenjiang County, and came back by boat. He saw a fisherman catch a pair of carp by the side of Baihua Lake, about a foot long. He bought them and brought them home. At that time, it was cold, so we warmed wine and grilled the fish to ward off the cold. After eating the fish, the bones were discarded. The maidservant said: 'One of the fish bones was black, it was iron.' He had a craftsman examine it, and it was indeed iron. So it was forged into this sword. Now facing calamity, trapped among the bandits. On the day the city fell, which would survive, the sword or the person? If this sword is lost, I will be lost too. If I am lost first, I do not know who will inherit the sword. This sword should be highly valued. One day, please remember it well for me." Yu died in the midst of the bandits, filled with sorrow and anger. On the day the city fell, our home was burned and looted, and the fate of the sword remains unknown. Therefore, I narrate his words to record them.

Volume 8 The Auspicious Peony In the spring of the Xin Hai year during the Dazhong Xiangfu era, Governor of Yizhou and Imperial Councillor Zhongzheng Ren hosted a flower-viewing banquet at the Daci Temple. At that time, a citizen named Wang presented a double-petaled peony, and Governor Ren immediately drew it. The common people and scholars who watched were packed all day. Since the Tang dynasty, there has been no such flower in Western Shu. In all paintings, it is only named the Luoyang flower. According to old sayings, it is called the wood peony. The name "peony" probably originated in the early Tianbao era. According to the *Yuyang Zazu* (Miscellaneous Notes from the Youyang Region), there were no poems about peonies in the collected works of literati during the Sui dynasty. The ten-volume work on horticulture from the Sui dynasty also does not mention the peony. By the time of the Shu kingdom, the peony was transplanted from the regions around Luoyang and Hanzhong. The Shu kingdom constructed ponds and lakes, built terraces and pavilions, and had all kinds of unusual and exotic plants, peculiar stones, and tall bamboo. The garden was named "Xuanhua." The high-ranking officials and meritorious subjects competed in building mansions, reaching the utmost in luxury and beauty. At that time, a maternal uncle named Xu Yanqiong newly constructed a mansion, which was elaborately carved, grand, and luxurious, with all kinds of flowers and trees present, except for the peony.

It was said that there was a single tree of red peony in a Buddhist monastery at Dongcheng Village, Qinzhou. Someone offered gold and silk to have it taken, and a square pit ten feet on each side was dug to extract it. It was placed in a wooden chest and transported over three thousand li to Shu, where it was planted in a new residence. The flower bloomed rarely, and when the ruler visited, he admired the magnificence of the house, which rivaled the grandeur of a palace. He ordered the character "Meng" to be inscribed on the pillars, for the common saying was that "Meng" meant something unworthy. The following year, when the Later Tang dynasty launched a campaign, Meng Zhixiang rushed from Taiyuan to Shu, and thus the omen was fulfilled. Prince Zongyu, the False Tongzheng, also built pavilions, terraces, and ponds at Qingyuan to the east of the northern gate, planting flowers and bamboo in rows. Springs and rocks wound around the garden, and a winding stream with nine bends was created, making it the finest garden of its time. Only one bud of the peony had just begun to open, and the prince, accompanied by his relatives, held a banquet with courtesans and musicians to admire it. However, the flower had already been broken by one of the female courtesans, and the prince was furious, wanting to execute her. His wife advised him, saying: "This courtesan is skilled in playing the pipa; let her perform music in front of the steps as part of the celebration." The prince's anger somewhat subsided, showing just how rare and precious the flower was. Later, the Meng family extensively planted peonies in Xuanhua Garden, naming them "peony flowers." Outside of this, the Lichun variety and those from Lizhou were slightly different.

A Letter to the Peacock My foolish friend, Left Attendant Xin Yixian, serves as an inspector of the regions of Rongyi, Lian, and Bai. He has sent me a young peacock. These regions are far to the southwest, and people in the north have never seen such a bird. It is truly wonderful. The mountains of the regions under his jurisdiction are filled with peacocks. The females have short tails without the golden luster, while the males have large, green tails that are shiny and eye-catching. Peacocks are very attached to their tails. When they want to rest, they first choose a place that will showcase their tail. People in the south capture peacocks by setting up nets. They wait for heavy rain, when the peacock's tail becomes wet and heavy, making it difficult for them to fly. When first captured, the peacock wants to spread its wings but fears damaging its tail. Even when captured, the peacock continues to protect its tail until death. There are people who capture peacocks alive to take their tails. They hide with a knife in a quiet, dense bamboo area and wait for the peacock to pass, then quickly cut off its tail. If they do not cut it quickly, and the peacock turns back, its tail loses its shine. Therefore, live peacocks are more valuable. Peacock feathers are used in women's jewelry and fans. Sometimes, live peacocks are fed to people in the capital cities, similar to how geese and swans are fed. The taste of peacock meat is similar to that of geese and swans. In the South Sea, there was a scholar who once kept a peacock. His servant reported that a snake had wrapped around the peacock, fearing it might poison it. The scholar ordered a rescue, and when the servant returned, he merely laughed.

A scholar was angered by something, and his servant reported: "When snakes and peacocks occasionally obtain their eggs, if a hen incubates them, they will hatch. Their name is: Protector. In the first year, they grow green feathers; in the second year, they grow tails with small fire eyes; in the third year, they grow large fire eyes. Their tail is thus completed. Peacocks often spread their tails in bright weather, looking back at themselves, which is called 'morning tail.' A room should be built with windows facing the bright direction, illuminated from east to west, with a wooden frame inside for them to rest. They love to face the light and do not stay on the ground. Feed them with rice, grain, beans, and wheat, and do not let them lack water, just like raising chickens. In autumn and summer, have servants pick live insects like grasshoppers and crickets from the fields to feed them. When feeding, bring them to the hall so they get used to seeing guests. In hot summer, if they suffer from eye pain, use a goose feather tube to apply a little fresh oil, and wash with newly drawn water. If their eyes do not open, feed them small fish and shrimp, otherwise they will starve and suffer. Give them a little of the soft (material). They prefer cool and cold food. If there is more food, it is better, but do not give them salty or sour things. Eating these will reduce their spirit and make their feathers dull. After being tamed for a long time, they will chase and peck at women and children wearing colorful clothes and ribbons. Or during pleasant times and beautiful scenery, hearing the sounds of string and bamboo instruments and singing, they will spread their wings and tails, look around, and dance as if with intention."

Teng Chushi Teng Chushi Changyou, styled Shenghua, specializes in painting and calligraphy. The inscriptions on the Great Sage Benevolent Temple, the Manjushri Pavilion, the Samantabhadra Pavilion, and the Heavenly Flower Auspicious Image are all his work. He embodies and depicts things, and his skillful and exquisite standard or quality is recorded in the *Catalogue of Famous Paintings*. Teng Chushi's residence is in the northeast corner of the city, surrounded by bamboo and trees, with a serene and secluded atmosphere. His garden and pond have pavilions and terraces, and he plants a variety of flowers and fruits. All his methods of mounding up soil around plants and planting them are well-defined, and he uses powdered herbs as food. At eighty-five years old, he has never stopped practicing painting and calligraphy. In his hall, there hangs a large whiteboard on which he has written the names and qualities of over a hundred flowers and plants in his garden. There are also exotic and rare plants from distant places, which he likely uses for his artistic inspiration. In his garden, there is a persimmon tree. In the summer, it can shelter more than ten people, spreading out like a canopy, and there is no feeling of heat. The persimmon tree has seven virtues, making it very suitable for planting: it is long-lived, provides ample shade, has no nests for birds, no pests, bears good fruit, has a strong root system, and its autumn leaves turn red and are beautiful to admire.

There is a pond in a basin, initially buried with a large basin filled with fine soil mixed with fine materials, and a small amount of finely chopped green onions and wine lees added. The depth is about two feet or more, and it is soaked with water. In early spring, coarse lotus roots with more than three joints and four or five damaged stems are dug up and buried in deep mud near the bottom of the basin. By the time of the spring equinox, the leaves grow, and flowers bloom in the same year. The lotus has four beauties: its roots as vegetables, its flowers as ornaments, its fruits as fruits, and its leaves as ladles. With these four beauties, the lotus surpasses other aquatic plants like duckweed, algae, and water chestnuts in ponds and pavilions. In the garden, there are bamboo plants that grow in clusters, with roots not far from the mother plant, hence named '慈' (filial). There is fishing pole bamboo, which is weak and droops to the ground at the tip. There is silk bamboo, with fine, green leaves and thin, purple stems, also called '墨竹' (ink bamboo). There is paired green bamboo, with a yellow body and green nodes that are paired, hence named '对青' (paired green). There is bitter bamboo, with dense, shady leaves and fragrant, green shoots when they are high. There is pillar bamboo, lush and graceful, fitting well in pavilions and terraces, none of these types of bamboo are out of place. Commonly, bamboo planted on May 13th often dries up due to intense sunlight, but in the garden, bamboo is planted before and after the August social day. This month, the sky is often overcast and the soil is moist, which is the best time for bamboo to establish roots. Whenever you want to move bamboo, first dig a wide pit and mix water with fine soil to make a thin mud. Then dig up the bamboo, cut off the large roots on all sides, bind the roots together with rope, and lift it carefully without disturbing the root hairs.

In planting bamboo, the soil should be carried into the pit and placed in the mud, ensuring that the mud surrounds and fills it completely. Then, shake it east-west and north-south, allowing the mud to penetrate the root area. Cover it with fine soil, but do not let the soil block the bamboo roots. If the bamboo grows taller, cut off the top leaves and tie it to a bamboo frame to prevent it from being shaken by the wind and dying. Each clump should be spaced about two feet apart, and there is no need to dig deeply; just step on it with one foot to compact the soil. This method ensures that the bamboo shoots will grow quickly the following year. It is best to plant bamboo in the soft soil of the northeast garden, as bamboo tends to grow roots to the southwest. Do not water it frequently, as too much water can cause it to rot. In the garden, there is a pear tree named Chahu, with a circumference of one foot. When picking the pears, first place them in a cloth bag, as they will break if dropped. There is also a golden peach, deep yellow in color, with a red and green pit like gold. Its taste is the best among peaches. There is a loquat tree whose fruit is as white as jade, with red spots on the sunny side, resembling patterns. Each fruit weighs about four ounces. The method of planting fruit trees involves cutting a straight branch from a good fruit tree during the period from the winter solstice to the start of spring, which should have a knee-like swelling as big as a thumb and be about two feet long. Insert this branch into the center of a taro bulb, dig the soil to make it wide, mix the mud to make it fine, cut about one sheng of fresh scallions, and stir them into the mud. Place the taro in the mud and cover it with fine soil, but do not make it too compact. This method will result in flowering in the same year and bearing fruit the following year, far surpassing the traditional method of planting seeds or grafting.

In all fruit grafting, first obtain wild tree seeds that are sour and astringent and not beautiful, and anything above the arm is suitable for grafting. Then, seek beautiful fruit branches, selecting those that will have crane-knee (鹤膝) and face the sun next year. The branches should not exceed two feet in length; if they do, they will be difficult to manage. When the time comes, cut them down and immediately insert them into a radish, in order to prevent the loss of their vitality. Ten days after the winter solstice and seven days before the beginning of spring, when the wild tree's bark is moist and the buds have not yet sprouted, is the right time. Cut the wild tree with a saw, leaving five to seven inches above the ground, and split the center open to a depth of about two inches. Take a beautiful branch, one or two, and insert them diagonally, without damaging the bark, into the cleft of the wild tree. The outer part should align with the wild tree's bark, and seal it tightly with cow dung mud. Wrap the grafting site with bamboo shoots and leaves, and tie it securely with hemp thread. Cover the top with yellow earth mud to form a mound, to prevent rainwater from penetrating. If any wild tree sprouts buds or leaves on the side, remove them immediately. If you follow this method, flowers will definitely bloom in the same year. I once followed this advice and planted trees, and all of them grew successfully. However, not understanding that the wild tree seeds are sour and astringent, while the crane-knee branches are sweet, grafting onto a sour and astringent tree, it is pushed by the sour and astringent energy, how could it then become sweet? The tree's vital energy cannot push the small branches to become sour and astringent along with it, why is that? It is said that the root cannot overcome the branch, and the nature of things is hard to understand. Today's people merely enjoy the shade of the branches and eat the delicious fruit, without seeking the sour and astringent qualities that are pushed by the tree.
